Benefit Narrative: There are occupancy schedules implemented through the BAS to control when equipment starts and stops, and to establish occupied and unoccupied temperature setpoints. Most spaces are in occupied mode from 5:00 a.m. to 3:00 p.m. on weekdays, with occupied and unoccupied temperature setpoints of 68°F and 62°F respectively. However, there are some spaces with slightly longer schedules and/or no unoccupiedtemperature setback. While there may be good reasons for these exceptions, consider implementing consistent schedules and setpoints throughout all zones.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Optimize control of Bus Engine Block Heater
Benefit Narrative: There are twenty-five (25) 1,500-watt electric block heaters for the school bus engines. It is possible the operation of these heaters can be optimized if they are currently being operated manually (e.g. plugged in at the end of the day and unplugged in the morning). By incorporating timers and/or thermostatic control, operating hours of the heaters could be reduced significantly. Incentives should be available from Efficiency Vermont for implementing this project.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Add monitoring and Control Capability for Exhaust Fans
Benefit Narrative: While most of the equipment in the building is visible and controlled through the building automation system, there are several exhaust fans that are not currently integrated into the system. The building operator expressed interest in adding status and command points for all exhaust fans, so that operation can be more easily monitored and scheduled. Additionally, the six exhaust fans that currently are being monitored and controlled have an operating schedule in the BAS of 4:00 a.m. ? 11:00 p.m. Consider tightening this schedule to reflect the hours when the building is occupied.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Ensure Tight Cosure of Unit Ventilators Outside Air Dampers
Benefit Narrative: Many of the unit ventilators have outside air dampers that do not close tightly, resulting in uncontrolled infiltration of outdoor air when the damper is commanded closed. Consider incorporating adjustment of outside air dampers as part of routine maintenance activities.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Raise Unit Ventilator Supply Air Tempeature Setpoints
Benefit Narrative: The supply air temperature setpoint for the unit ventilators is currently 70°F, while the unoccupied space temperature setpoint is 62°F. This may result in excessive fan run times during unoccupied periods due to the small temperature difference between the supply and room air. Consider raising the setpoint to 90°F.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Replace Dishwasher Electric Booster Heater with Propane Heater
Benefit Narrative: The dishwasher in the kitchen has a 3 kW electric resistance booster heater to elevate the temperature of the hot water. Replacing this with a propane heater could result in a peak demand reduction, as well as cost savings from switching to a cheaper fuel.

The school is on an electric tariff with relatively high demand charges that also includes a ratchet, meaning that the peak demand set during any particular month will determine the billed demand for the next eleven months. We recommend raising awareness among facility staff regarding this issue and implementing policies to avoid setting new peak demand levels (e.g. by operating electric kilns only during off-peak periods after 4:00pm). Since there is visibility into electric demand on the meter through the BAS, it is even possible to set alarms when the building demand rises above a certain threshold to alert the building operator.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Install Variable Speed Drive to Control Hot Water Pumos
Benefit Narrative: The existing hot water pumps operate at a constant speed regardless of the actual heating demand in the building. By slowing down the pumps to match the flow to the demand using a variable frequency drive, significant energy savings can be achieved. Efficiency Vermont programs assist with the cost of energy efficient equipment.

Many of the unit ventilators are original equipment from when the building was initially constructed in 1967. These are being replaced gradually as capital funds become available. Ensure that the most efficient units are selected at the time of replacement, which include electronically commutated (ECM) motors. Efficiency Vermont programs assist with the cost of energy efficient equipment.

Lighting in a few classrooms has already been upgraded to LED. We recommend continuing with LED lighting upgrades as funds become available, and for classrooms in perimeter areas, consider incorporating daylight dimming controls. Efficiency Vermont may assist with the cost of these replacements.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Calibrate CO2 Sensors to ensure proper ventilation
Benefit Narrative: Ventilation in most areas of the building is controlled to maintain a maximum carbon dioxide concentration of 1,500 ppm. Since CO2 sensors are prone to drifting out of calibration, we recommend implementing a program to periodically calibrate the CO2 sensors on an annual basis to ensure that proper ventilation is

Benefit: Benefit Name: Adjust Schedules in BAS to Reflect Actual Occupancy Patterns
Benefit Narrative: Building occupancy schedule in BAS is weekdays from 4 a.m. ? 3 p.m. The earliest teachers get to the building is around 6:00 a.m. and students arrive at 7:20 a.m. There is an occupancy schedule set for Wednesdays 3 -8 p.m. due to gym being used during this time, but schedule is being applied to entire building, not just gym AHU. Reccomend adjusting schedules in BAS to reflect actual occupancy patterns.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Return Hot Water Pump P-1 to Automatic Control
Benefit Narrative: Pump P-1 in boiler room was running with no control available through BAS, presumably due to a bad relay. Recommend repairing  or replace relay for hot water pump P-1 in boiler room and ensure automatic control through BAS is achieved.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Ensure Adequate Ventilation is being Provided
Benefit Narrative: Inadequate ventilation in server room.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Relocate Outside Aire Sensor, other rcx Maintenance Recommendations
Benefit Narrative: The outside air temperature sensor used in building automation control sequences is located on the northeast corner of the building. During morning hours, it is in the sun and reading higher than the actual temperature. This can affect operation of equipment with control sequences that reference the OAT point, including economizer control in unit ventilators, boiler enable control, and hot water supply temperature reset. Recommend relocating outside air temperature sensor to ensure it is shaded at all times so that the true temperature is being read by the building automation system. This also may help with some of the comfort issues in the building, since economizer operation may have been locked out mistakenly.

AHU-5 was initially observed to be in cooling mode, while the steam preheat coil 1/3 valve was also open and preheating the supply air. It was determined that the steam valve was leaking by, even when commanded to 100% closed. Savings can be attributed to two different issues for this measure: 1. The preheat coil 1/3 valve opening during mechanical cooling mode 2. The steam valve leaking by when commanded to the full closed positionThere are heating plant and cooling plant savings that will be realized for this measure. Issue 1, preheat coil valve opening during mechanical cooling, will be remedied with the straight to implementation phase work currently being done. Issue 2, steam valve leak-by, can be resolved as part of the next implementation phase work by following the action below. Action: Adjust valve and actuator to close completely when commanded. If leak-by remains, repair or replace control valve so that there is no steam leak-by when valve is commanded to close.

All of the hospital?s AHUs currently rely on outside air damper positions as a means of controlling outdoor air flow. Damper position is never reflective of the actual outside air CFM, and when used in this manner typically results in over ventilating and, in some cases, under ventilating the spaces served. A contemporary approach is to use a device to measure the outside air flow and control the damper to achieve an airflow that meets the requirements of ASHRAE 170-2013 or the exhaust air CFM requirements of the spaces they currently serve, whichever is greater.Action:. A TAB contractor should do an existing exhaust airflow study to verify the exhaust airflows in the AHU-5 area; Verify required minimum OA of AHU-5 based on results of exhaust air TAB study; A pressure study of all areas with pressure differential requirements should be conducted and make final AHU airflow adjustments as needed to maintain pressure requirements.

column2: Leland and Gray Union Middle & High School RCx
Benefit Narrative: During the qualification phase BAS review, it was believed that some terminal reheat coils associated with AHU-3 and AHU-6 were heating supply air when not enabled by the BAS. After testing it was determined that this was not an issue. A sample of reheats associated with AHU-6 and AHU-3 were tested by recording air temperature upstream and downstream of the reheat coil using a calibrated test instrument. All reheats tested did not exhibit any signs of unintended heating. No action is required as part of this measure, however, the reheat coil serving Echo Room 123 was found to not be reheating air while the reheat coil valve was 100% open. This issue could be due the reheat coil being manually isolated. The coil should be examined to determine the cause of the issue.

column2: Southwestern Vermont Medical Center Chiller RCx
Benefit: Benefit Name: Unthrottle Balancing Valves on Pump Discharge
Benefit Narrative: ?	Condenser water pump P-5 has throttled triple duty valve

column2: Southwestern Vermont Medical Center Chiller RCx
Benefit: Benefit Name: Operate Cooling Tower Cells in Parallel
Benefit Narrative: ?	Currently each cooling tower cell is dedicated to a chiller, so that if only one chiller is operating, only one cell and cooling tower fan are operating. It is more efficient to operate both cells in parallel due to the increased surface area for heat rejection and the energy savings achieved at lower fan speeds

column2: Southwestern Vermont Medical Center Chiller RCx
Benefit: Benefit Name: Optimize Chilled Water Plant Control Sequences
Benefit Narrative: Pump P-5 serving condenser water loop for CH-1 was observed to be operating in HAND, while CH-1 was not operating at the time  Recommend Optimize control sequences for chilled water plant operation, including: Chilled water supply temperature control, Secondary chilled water pump speed control, Condenser water supply temperature control, Condenser water pump control, Cooling tower control

column2: Southwestern Vermont Medical Center Chiller RCx
Benefit: Benefit Name: Release BAS Overrides and Return Equipment to AUTO Control
Benefit Narrative: Pumps P-1, P-3, and P-4 speeds were overridden to 100% through the BAS (although P-1 and P-3 were off at the time)

column2: Southwestern Vermont Medical Center Chiller RCx
Benefit Narrative: AHU-1 and AHU-2 do not currently have any airflow setback schedules, and the spaces served by them have no temperature setback schedules. A setback for these air handlers and associated spaces can be accomplished by programming an occupied / unoccupied schedule and engineering a system with occupancy sensors so the users do not have to activate it manually should the need to use the ORs and associated spaces arise during off hours. In addition, a wall mounted manual override button would be included in each OR and perhaps at the nurses? station(s) as a fail-safe. The occupancy command of the air handlers would be tied together, so the OR suite (AHU-2) could never go into an occupied mode without also enabling the occupied mode of AHU-1 and vice versa.Action: Develop an unoccupied sequence for the OR suite and surgical services areas and engineer system for detecting occupancy in operating rooms. After engineering is complete, implement unoccupied sequences, and schedule unoccupied times through schedule settings on BAS.

It was observed during the development investigation that several of the air handlers had fan speeds that were higher during minimum outside air mode than when in an economizer mode. This indicates that there was likely a restriction on the fan system during minimum outside air mode due to the control damper positions. The ASHRAE Guideline 36 mixing box sequence can reduce the external pressure on the fan system due to the control dampers. This sequence will yield fan energy savings when the AHU is in minimum outside air mode.Outdoor airflow stations are required to implement this sequence. An added benefit of outdoor air flow stations is that they also provide monitoring and trending of AHU OA CFM.Action:
1. Install outdoor air flow stations (AFS) to control, monitor, and trend AHU OA CFM.
2. Reprogram AHU control damper sequences to use installed air flow stations to maintain verified minimum outside air CFM.

It was observed that no energy savings will be associated with this measure. This measure is currently being implemented with the first phase of RCx measures as it was part of the new economizer control and temperature control sequences being implemented with that phase of work.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Unnecessary or redundant MERV air filters in AHUs
Benefit Narrative: ?	Unnecessary or redundant MERV air filters within AHUs can significantly reduce energy efficiency and increase O&M burden with no additional benefits to air quality. Cx Associates identified unnecessary intermediate MERV air filters in the design of every AHU

Benefit: Benefit Name: Change three-way valves in CRAC units to two-way
Benefit Narrative: We suggested changing three-way valves in CRAC units to two-way

Benefit: Benefit Name: Reduce design water pressure drop through chiller
Benefit Narrative: During design review for the new Central Utility Plant (CUP) chiller, Cx Associates identified an opportunity to improve energy efficiency by reducing the design water pressure drop through the condenser and evaporator of the new 2000 Ton chiller.  The chiller was reselected by the design team and combined pressure drop was reduced by approximately 10 ft, which results in an estimated annual pumping energy savings of 25,000 kWh.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Return Pump Operation to AUTO and Release BAS Oerrides
Benefit Narrative: While reviewing BAS functionality, we found that the pump serving Zone 8 (P-37) was showing a status of ON even though it was commanded OFF, indicating the pump is operating in HAND. Additionally, the zone valve position for Zone 6 was found to be overridden to 20%. . This issue may also be related to the fact that the Zone 8 controlling sensor is actually located in Zone 9 (Issue #7).

Functional testing was performed to confirm proper snowmelt sensor operation by spraying sensors with water and observing whether sensor status changed from ?Dry? to ?Wet? in the BAS. All sensors were found to be functioning properly except for the sensor for Zone 9, which was reading as ?Wet? regardless of the actual conditions. This would cause the zone to always operate in snowmelt mode whenever the system is enabled, leading to increased pump operation and propane consumption.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Reduce Snowmelt Mode Slab Temperature Setpoints
Benefit Narrative: During review of BAS settings, we discovered that two of the zones (Zone 8 and Zone 9) had elevated snowmelt mode slab temperature setpoints of 70°F and 60°F respectively. Again, this is likely related to the issue regarding improper sensor location for Zone 8. At some point during the winter, the building operator manually adjusted the setpoints in the BAS in an effort to clear any snow from the zone surfaces. These setpoints should be lowered to 45°F, similar to the other zones.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Replace Missing Roof Mainfold Cabinet Door and Bleached Piping
Benefit Narrative: The cabinet containing one of the roof zone supply manifolds is missing its door, resulting in exposure to sunlight and bleached piping. PEX tubing typically has a sunlight exposure rating of 20 hours, so the integrity of these pipes is most likely compromised.

column2: VTC SHAPE Fitness & Campus Center Phase 2 RCx
Benefit Narrative: Currently, the AHUs serving the campus center are programmed to introduce a minimum outside airflow whenever the spaces are occupied and will increase outside air if necessary to maintain CO2 levels below 900 ppm. However, most of the units appear to be introducing excessive amounts of outside air, resulting in unnecessary conditioning of supply air. Recommend ensuring that only the minimum necessary outside aire required for ventilation is being introduced.

column2: VTC SHAPE Fitness & Campus Center Phase 2 RCx
Benefit Narrative: The following issues were observed for the air handling unit serving the gymnasium: The supply and return fans on the gym air handling unit are equipped with variable frequency drives (VFDs), but are currently operated manually as constant speed fans; The AHU is currently in occupied mode 24/7; The outside air sensor is out of calibration and consistently reading 10-20°F higherthan actual. This is preventing the unit from taking advantage of free cooling in economizer mode; Several control points are manually overridden in the BAS, including supply and return fan commands, outside air damper position, and outside air temperature. Reccomendation is to apply an occupancy schedule to the gym AHU and operate the unit as a single zone variable air volume system under automatic control.

column2: VTC SHAPE Fitness & Campus Center Phase 2 RCx
Benefit Narrative: The pool air handling unit supplies conditioned air to the natatorium to maintain temperature and relative humidity setpoints. Humidity can be controlled to the extent possible by varying the amount of outside air introduced, but there is no mechanical dehumidification present. Currently, the supply fan operates at 100% speed at all times regardless of the actual load, while the return fan speed is set to 35.5 Hz (59%).When conditions allow, reduce supply and return flow while maintaining appropriate conditions in the natatorium. As recommended by ASHRAE3, supply a minimum of 4-6 air changes per hour (ACH) and maintain pool room at slightly negative pressure in order to prevent chloramine odor migration to adjacent spaces. From our calculations, a supply fan speed of 70% corresponds to around six (6) ACH.

column2: VTC SHAPE Fitness & Campus Center Phase 2 RCx
Benefit Narrative: The 15-horsepower pool filter pump  operates at 48 Hz (80% speed, 7.7 kW) continuously 24/7. The fixed speed was presumably set at the time of VFD installation in order to meet minimum required water exchange rates, and saves significant energy compared to operating the pump at full speed.  Consider reducing the pool filter pump speed to 66% speed (40 Hz) at night when the pool is unoccupied. Contaminant load is much lower when there are no occupants in the pool, and a slightly reduced water turnover rate during these times should not significantly impact water quality. Building operators could experiment with different speeds and unoccupied durations in order to arrive at an optimal configuration and maximize potential savings.

column2: VTC SHAPE Fitness & Campus Center Phase 2 RCx
Benefit Narrative: Currently, the pool stays uncovered when not in use, resulting in significant evaporation and associated heat loss, along with increased humidity in the natatorium. Recommend installing a pool cover and establishing a daily procedure for covering and uncovering the pool.

column2: VTC SHAPE Fitness & Campus Center Phase 2 RCx
Benefit: Benefit Name: Budget for Replacement of Corroded Snowmelt Sensors
Benefit Narrative: We observed that several snowmelt sensors are suffering from corrosion. While the sensors were operating properly at the time of our site visit, they may cease functioning at any time and should be replaced soon.

column2: UVM Kalkin Hall Renovations and Additions Cx
Benefit Narrative: Facilities staff indicated that the snowmelt system suffers from balancing and flow issues. System balancing will ensure that all zones receive the required flow.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Optimize Control Sequence for Pump P-21
Benefit Narrative: The pump serving the snowmelt loop header is equipped with a VFD and designed to modulate speed to maintain a return water temperature of 125°F. However, a trend data review showed that the pump speed does not appear to be modulating appropriately. This will be investigated further during the Development Phase, and a new sequence of operations will be written and implemented, potentially controlling pump speed based on differential pressure rather than return water temperature.

Kitchen MAU are extremly dirty filters but BAS was indicating they are clean, sugginting that the BAS filter pressure switch is broken or out of callibration. Recommend perforing preventative maintenance check, including changing the filters on the MAU and ERV.

The MAU was activly bypassing the heat exchanger core, however, the graphic showed it not in bypass. The locker room ventilation appears to be poor due to the ERV running at a very low speed. The odor in the rooms was very prominent and there is condensation on t he doors, sugessing the humidity and room air changes might be poorly controled. Recomend rebalancing ERV to improve i ndoor air quality in locker rooom and prevent condensation on doors.

Rooms on the third floor were designed to have one thermostat control (2x) heat pumps. This results in the thermostats being parallel wired to both heat pumps. This caused fuses to blow in the heat pumps because two independent 24 val control sources were shorted together through the thermostat relays. Recommend that the existing thermostats serving the guest suits need isolation relays added. this measure will reduce runtime on the singular heat pump running t omaintain the load of the two installed heat pumps.

Poor thermostat placement causing the two heat pumps to be able to simultaneously heat and cool the same space.  It is recommeded that the existing thermostats serving the guest bedrooms should be removed and those heat pumps should be interlocked in the same a the 3rd floor using isolation relays.

The tower was completely plugged up and had not been cleaned in over a year. The result of that was a poor heat rejection delta (2.5F).  Also found the setpiont were not coordinated with the heat pumps factory requirements. Recommend installing a new well on the return pupe and moving sensors into the new well.

The high temp heating loop is served by a pair of 3 horsepower pumps. They pump into a modulating three way mixing valve which diverts injects heat into hybrid heat pump loops. During cold weather events, this configuration can result in series pumping , meaning that the outlet of one pump is feeding directly into the inlet of another pump. Recommend modifying the pipe configuraation of the 3 way valve to remove the series pumping situaltion.

The controlling temperature sensor for the cooling tower is improperly installed. It appears that during construction the sensor thermowell was installed in the cooling towere supply pipe but should have been instaled in the return pipe. The sensor was removed and installed in a thermowell for a visual thermometer on the tower return line. The probelm is that the diameter of the thermometers well is much larger than the sensors well and the sensor element is making poor contact with the thermowell. Recommend installing the correct sensor well for the cooling loop in the proper location on the piping.

This measure would involve developing upgraded control sequences that would provide better temperature control of the Bulldog heat pump condenser loop during shoulder seasons. We suspect that there is a significant opportunity to optimize the loop control to reduce boiler, cooling tower and downstream heat pump runtimes while improving comfort.

This measure develops a guest room thermostat upgrade that would allow the Bulldog heat pumps to be networked. This measure can be developed independently from D-1, however, there will be an increased benefit to developing both measures together.  Cx associates will then design and commission a networked thermostat control solution for the hotel. This solution would allow the guest room thermostats to take advantage of the Maestro integration that is currently in use at Hotel Jay to set back the heat pumps during unoccupied periods. A secondary benefit of this measure will be allowing the central plant to better react to the building demand from the heat pumps in real time. This results in reduced energy consumed by the central plant since it will no longer be anticipating the demand using outside air temperature, but rather the calls for heating and cooling from the heat pumps themselves.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Add Occupancy Schedule to BAS for ERV-1
Benefit Narrative: No apparent schedule implemented in the BAS to control equipent. Even though ERV-1 supply and exhaust fans are equpped with VFD's they run constantly at 100% speed.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Add Occupancy Schedule to BAS for MAU-1
Benefit Narrative: No apparent schedule implemented in the BAS to control equipent. MAU-1 supply fan is overridden to 75% for all hours.

Adjust fan coil unit controls so that FCU fans shut off during the cooling season rather than running in an attempt to meet cooling setpoints. If cooling will not be added at any point in the future, consider removing DX cooling coils from fan coil units, as they currently serve only to restrict flow and cannot provide cooling.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Interlock Room Occupancy Schedules with Booking System
Benefit Narrative: Interlock Room Occupancy Schedules with Booking System
Investigate the capabilities of a Winona path lighting controller and potential to optimize exterior lighting control.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Optimize Hot Water supply Temperature Reset Control
Benefit Narrative: The differential pressure in the hot water loop is a fixed value of 13psi. There may be an opportunity to decrease this or permorm a reset based on the number of open valves in the loop. Investigate the potential for optimizing hot water supply temperature reset control to meet the building heating load, shifting the reset schedule down to maximize the hours during which the boilers operate in condensing range.
The monthly electric energy consumption baseload is aroudnd 10,000kWh with a significant increase during the summer months due to cooling energy use and golf cart battery charging.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Insure Proper Exhaust for Batter Charging Area
Benefit Narrative: Ensure exhaust fan (EF-1) and supply fan (SF-1) serving the golf cart storage area operate whenever batteries are charging in order to provide adequate ventilation air.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Install CCHP to Supplement Electric Resistance Heat
Benefit Narrative: The seven existing electric storage heaters can provide approximately 95,000 Btu/h at maximum capacity when the fans are operating to draw heat from the storage core. Through discussion with the manufacturer (Steffes) it was determined that the heaters output approximately 24,000 Btu/h when fully charged and are not actively outputting heat via the internal fan. These findings were used to develop the proposed systems and sequences for the heat pump systems.

We found that a couple of the snowmelt zones had their controlling sensors located in an adjacent zone, which caused problems with snowmelt control. One potential solution to ensure that the system operates effectively is to add programming to interlock the operation of zones where this is the case. For example, if the sensor for Zone 2 or Zone 4 detects moisture, then also enable operation for Zone 3. Similarly, if the sensor for Zone 7 or Zone 9 detects moisture, enable operation for Zone 8.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Reduce VAV Box Minimum Flow Rate
Benefit Narrative: VAV box minimum flow rates were set as high as 80% of the maximum box flow. Unclear why it was set so high. We reccommended reducing minimum flow rates which would result in fan energy savings as well as heating and cooling savings.
Rooftop unit supply fans currently operate to maintain a fixed static pressure of 1.4-1.5 in w.g. at all times. While this static pressure may be necessary during times of peak cooling demand, the s

Analysis of 15-minute electric interval data suggested that mechanical cooling is operating at outside air temperatures as low as 50°F. The energy recovery wheel bypass dampers in the rooftop units may not be operating properly, resulting in undesirable energy recovery during mild weather. During the Development Phase, we will analyze trend data over a wide range of outside air conditions and perform on-site testing of the RTUs to ensure systems are achieving maximum free cooling potential.

The facility manager mentioned humidity is not controlled well in some areas, which can be a serious problem for finished food products. We believe facility operation would benefit from a thorough commissioning of the AAON rooftop units, including functional performance test to ensure dehumidification sequence is working properly

Benefit: Benefit Name: Confirm Calibration of Temperature and Humidity Sensors
Benefit Narrative: Temperature and relative humidity sensors can drift out of calibration over time, resulting in increased energy use, poor control of interior conditions, or both. Relative humidity sensors are especially prone to drift. Data loggers were deployed to a few spaces to record temp and relative humidity with the intent of comparing to BAS readings and determine how well sensors are calibrated. Unfortunatly data logger memory ran out so no overlapping period was obtained.We plan to test sensor calibration further during the Development Phase. We also recommend having the BAS contractor add 15-minute trending intervals to all BAS points for 1 year to ensure that when/if future retrocommissioning development activities are undertaken, sufficient historical data exists to inform this process.

Currently, the CaptiveAire kitchen exhaust hoods are manually and are not visible or controlled through the BAS. When the building was these were operating in an uncontrolled fashion for several months, resulting in energy consumption. We recommend adding fan start/stop and status points to the that these can be monitored and an alarm issued if they are found to be running continuously.

The cooling tower has basic controls, including a fixed speed 10 HP sump motor and a basin heater.  The cooling tower was reported to be rejecting heat in January and February of 2016. Replacing these factory controls with programmable BAS controllers may present an opportunity to further refine cooling tower operation and reduce heat rejection in winter months.  Development of the cooling tower measures, including heat rejection, might identify opportunity to refine the cooling tower sequence of operations and the condenser water system operation.  This should include metering of the cooling tower and loops serving the heat pumps.

Given the mechanical proximity of the condenser water loop and the pool heating loop, there may be an energy savings opportunity around waste heat.  The pool and hot tub have a 3,000MBH boiler dedicated to heating them and the cooling tower rejects up to 2,640MBH.  The cooling tower could be bypassed, and then the building heat rejected into the outdoor pool and hot tub loops as the first stage of condenser water heat rejection.  With the addition of a heat exchanger and some additional controls, the waste heat from air conditioning the hotel could be used to offset the propane usage for summer pool heating. Development of this measure, including metering heat rejected to the cooling towers and pool heat consumption, is included in order to determine feasibility and more accurately refine the savings estimates of this measure.

There is little integration with the BAS and the pool system.  The pool is suspected to be contributing to the high EUI of the building (from propane consumption).  This measure is to disaggregate its impact on Burke?s propane consumption.  Given the previous opportunity for heat rejection from the heat pump system, this study of the pool controls and operation would shed light on further potential opportunities to reduce propane consumption.

Due to the reported behavior of the snow melt system, the system should be fully tested and metered.  Snow melt is also suspected of being one of the contributing factors to the building?s high EUI.  This measure would develop systematic testing of the snow melt operation in response to snow fall as well as system sensor accuracy.  The sequence of operations will be reviewed and an optimized where applicable.

It was reported that the Telkonet thermostats are integrated into the BAS.  Using the occupancy data associated with this system, it may be possible to interlock ERVs with the heat pumps downstream in such a way that they would only run if a guest was occupying a room.  This measure will investigate the BAS and thermostat integration further to determine how much hardware (if any) and software would be required.  Metering of the ERV coupled with BAS trend data review is included to further refine savings estimates.  Our current estimate assumes one day of programming is required to interlock these two systems over BACnet.

column2: Trudell Consulting Engineers Blair Park Rd RCx
Benefit Narrative: While examining settings on the single operating Nest thermostat, we found that the heating and cooling temperature setpoints were not being set back during unoccupied hours. This measure was implemented during our site visit and should result in modest heating and cooling energy savings. Unoccupied heating temperature setpoint was set to 64°F and unoccupied cooling temperature setpoint was set to 83°F.

column2: Trudell Consulting Engineers Blair Park Rd RCx
Benefit Narrative: Condensing unit coil fins are fouled, hampering heat rejection and reducing efficiency.Clearing the fins of dirt and debris will increase heat transfer effectiveness and reduce cooling energy.

column2: Trudell Consulting Engineers Blair Park Rd RCx
Benefit: Benefit Name: Undergo Comprehensive Envelope and HVAC Renovation
Benefit Narrative: Capital investment is required in order to meaningfully improve the building?s comfort problems. The building owner recognizes this and has already begun pursuing various projects, including: Replacing existing windows on the south façade with high performance windows and reducing the total glazed area. Implementing effective shading elements such as horizontal overhangs and/or vertical fins should also be investigated. Many of the findings identified in the building envelope assessment (Appendix B) will also reduce heating and cooling loads and help with comfort issues if implemented.Replacing the existing HVAC system with a high efficiency system capable of serving multiple thermal zones. Two possibilities include a variable refrigerant flow (VRF) system with multiple indoor fan coil units, or several mini-split heat pumps.As part of any HVAC renovation, ensure provisions are included to provide mechanical ventilation to the building during occupied hours, such as a dedicated outdoor air system with energy recovery.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Recommission Sensor and Varify Proper Balance
Benefit Narrative: There are a number of chronic comfort issues reported by building occupants. In one office, airflow was at a relatively high velocity and blowing on occupants. Further, the thermostat was located close to an open doorway next to an adjacent zone, resulting in an inaccurate representation of the office zone being controlled. We recommend calibrating and/or relocating poorly placed sensors in order to allow controls to more accurately sense zone conditions. Further, balance verification and/or diffuser throw pattern adjustment may be able to reduce comfort issues by reducing excess airflow at common occupant locations. We also recommend verifying the control sequence during heat-add mode for the propane boiler?given the fast rate of consumption described by the staff, the geo loop may not be properly disengaged during heat-add.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Interlock Heating in Contemplative Space with Occupancy
Benefit Narrative: The heat was on in the ?Lens? building contemplative space, which is an out-building directly adjacent to CAPA?s main structure. The space is equipped with a mechanical remote skylight/shutter which was found open, despite the space being served by hot water heat, which was on. Assuming glycol concentration in the heating loop can be confirmed as sufficient for worst case winter outside air conditions, we recommend adding an interlock to the skylight which disables zone heat when the skylight is open to prevent wasted heating energy.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Enable Hot Gas Reheat for Humidity Control
Benefit Narrative: The geothermal terminal heat pumps are equipped with DX reheat coils, which can be utilized for dehumidification. The controls required for this option to function were not installed at the time of construction, however humidity control in the space is often an issue?in particular, without dehumidification control, radiant cooling, which is the primary cooling source in some areas, has been disabled due to issues with condensation and related slip hazards. It is critical for radiant cooling systems that either air is dehumidified to have a dewpoint lower than the slab temperature, or the slab is tightly controlled to not drop below the dewpoint of the space air.  At present, the heat pumps, which are the only potential means of dehumidification, cannot provide this essential function.
