Mylan RCx – 20 Houghton St Office Building

Benefit Narrative: With outdoor air temperatures in the mid 80?s, when we enabled the snowmelt controls, hot water began to run out to the slab. The slab was well above a winter slab idle temperature. While there is a slab mounted temperature/moisture sensor, it appears that the controls are not functioning correctly. The result is that whenever the system is enabled, heat is sent to the slab in a minimally controlled manner. This likely represents a large energy cost. The slab should be controlled to operate with the following sequence: When OAT is < 40F, enable snowmelt control and maintain idle slab temperature of 34F. If moisture is detected by slab sensor, slab setpoint goes to active, and maintains 44F slab temp.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Turn off Distribution Pump in Summer
Benefit Narrative: A circulation pump in City Hall was found to be operating, meaning it was circulating hot water throughout the Fire Station during the summer months. This will lead to wasted heating energy during the summer operating period and unnecessary summer heating of the basement. We recommend adding controls to disable this circulation pump automatically based on schedule (e.g. May through September) and/or by outdoor air temperature lockout at 60F (adj).

The hot water loop running through the basement of the station is 1-1/2? steel and is substantially uninsulated. The ľ? copper snowmelt loop piping running within the basement is also uninsulated. Significant heat loss can occur due to this uninsulated piping. While some of this heat acts to offset load on the equipment conditioning the space (Modine type unit heaters), at times the waste heat is likely exceeding the requirements of the space and is not useful. Insulation of this piping should provide a simple payback period of less than 1 year.

While on site, we noticed the distribution pump for summer hot water was operating at 100% speed (60 hz). The differential pressure (DP) setpoint was satisfied, however the pump was generating much more pressure than required to meet setpoint. The DP reading on the BAS was found to be invalid, which was causing the pumps to operate at full speed. The sensor issue was resolved by TCV and the pump is now operating below full speed. We performed a rough savings calculation and determined that the energy savings from addressing this issue resulted in 5,300 kWh of energy savings, and an associated cost savings of $750 to the City.

During our walkthrough, we found a few places where insulation was missing on piping at the building service entrance; at these locations, heat loss would end up on the City?s side of the BTU meter. Overall the delivery efficiency of the district loop during summer months appears to be roughly 89%, which is fairly good, though for a brand new installation could be still improved. We?ve estimated a 3% summer fuel reduction could be achieved by improving insulation in the service entrance locations and making a minor touch-up in the City Room.

Benefit: Benefit Name: High-Efficiency Boiler Retrofit, with Summer SWT Reset
Benefit Narrative: For the long term, we recommend considering a higher efficiency generating source for summer heating, which would be most economically viable during an upgrade. At present, the boiler plant is operating in the 80% efficiency range, which is reasonable for a non-condensing hot water boiler. While a right-sized non-condensing fuel oil fired boiler may operate at 86% efficiency, the actual efficiency revealed by the meter data is lower, likely due to the oversized nature of the existing boilers, and associated cycling to meet part load conditions. Upgrading the boiler system is capital intensive, and as such, this measure has a long payback on its own due to high capital costs and modest savings. However, at end of life replacement, increasing the plant efficiency could be very cost effective.

There are a number of metal halide fixtures on the exterior of the building, in the hallway between the offices/lab and the Process Room, and over the process room floor. According to operators, the process room lighting is not utilized except during certain maintenance activities, but it is old and could reap the maintenance savings benefits of switching to LEDs. Due to relatively low run hours, replacement on the basis of energy savings alone will not be cost effective?however replacement upon failure, or while other electrical work is underway may result in cost effective approach. Note: Since the completion of the project walkthrough, the hallway fixtures have already been relamped to LED by the Owner?s electrician. Further opportunities may still exist with outdoor lighting.

The heating plant consists of an oil-fired boiler, which generates 180F hot water year-round. Hot water needs in the space are almost exclusively limited to heating season. At a minimum, hot water supply water temperature could be reduced.

The dehumidification units in the Process Room and Bulk Chemical room are older, and high efficiency equipment is now available. While replacement prior to failure is not cost effective on an energy savings basis alone, when units begin to fail, we recommend replacing with higher efficiency units. Instead of 6-7 individual units, consider two rooftop or larger indoor units?one serving the process room and the other serving the bulk chemical and dry chemical rooms. Note that in addition to new equipment, consider a dehumidification control approach which monitors the raw process water temperature and resets the dehumidification setpoint based on this value This approach may result in energy savings, but will more importantly result in control which is more directly aimed at the objective, which is preventing corrosion and other consequences associated with sweating pipes.

Relocate chilled water supply dP sensor 2/3 of the distance down the longest or heaviest used pipe run and establish the new dP setpoint with Cx Associates.Notes: The existing dP sensor (labeled actual on BAS) was left in the same position. A new dP sensor (labeled ?added dP? on BAS) was installed further downstream of the pumps. This dP sensor is now controlling the loop but both values remain on the graphics. The setpoint moved from 18.1 psi to 17.5 psi and the pump reduced from 78% to 68% speed.
Install a power meter for monitoring the chiller.There is a next step to this work, which is the development of a kW/ton cooling algorithm that is described in the Open Items section of this memo.

Relocate the hot water pump dP sensor 2/3 out in the hot water system. This new location will enable the sensor to respond more accurately to the small changes in the system?s demand. Notes: The existing dP sensor (labeled actual on BAS) was left in the same position. A new dP sensor (labeled ?added dP? on BAS) was installed at AHU19. The original setpoints of the boiler system were 12 psi dP and we modified the pressure setpoint to be 8.6psi (8.0 psi on new sensor location). The original pumps speed was 84.2% and the pump slowed down to 66.5%. Over an hour, the heating requests went from 34 to 26, meaning the reduction in flow does not adversely affect the space temperatures.

Provide VFD, start-up and programming for the existing exhaust fan. Notes: The VFD was already programmed to operate at a maximum 50% speed upon a CO call. We checked the historical 10-day trend data and see that the garage has not gone above 42 ppm for more than 6 minutes under this operating sequence. The Department of Public Safety stipulates the fan must exhaust the garage to not exceed 50 ppm for 5-minutes. The current sequence is acceptable and provides energy savings. We did not change this setpoint.Although the BAS states the fan is ?off?, field verification shows that the fan is ?on? at a very slow speed and the exhaust louvers are closed. This new sequence increases the energy consumption of the garage and may need further investigation before ensuring it meets the updated code.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Office Areas Rcommissioning- Verify Zone/Control Mapping
Benefit Narrative: Recommend recommissioning office areas to ensure zones are correctly configured and that sequences/setpoints result in discrete zone control to whatever extent possible. This may mean that the perimeter radiation, if configured as only one zone, has one sequence in which it is only called to be enabled when OAT is below a particular setpoint. If this equipment is not on the BAS, this may mean either adding it to the BAS or adding a local controller with an OAT sensor. The individual constant volume reheats in each zone would provide conditioning during most of the year to each space, however when OAT drops below 20F (adj), the perimeter radiation would be called on to reduce window losses and improve comfort.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Service Areas Recommissioning- Review Setpoints and Controls
Benefit Narrative: Setpoints should be unified. These spaces are not on the BAS, and as such, we recommend either BAS integration or a smart(er) thermostat. A smart thermostat could result in better control (and ideally remote monitoring) of bus parking, service, and shop spaces. The snowmelt slab controls should also be verified and should be tightly controlled based on OA temperatures to minimize unnecessary runtime while ensuring readiness for ice melting when OATs fall suddenly.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Decouple DHW System to Achieve Condensing Boier Efficiency
Benefit Narrative: Due to the constant operating temperature of 150F-180F, which may have been driven by a domestic hot water heating priority configuration, the boilers are not condensing and are likely operating in the high 80% range. If domestic hot water can be generated using a standalone domestic hot water heater, the boiler supply water temperature could be reduced based on a reset schedule, such that during most of the year, the boilers could operate at optimum efficiency. This would result in a 6-9% fuel cost/energy savings. The existing domestic hot water heating tank could be left in place as a lower temperature DHW preheat system, with an electric heat pump water heater piped in series to provide final trim. Given that the mezzanine space is always warm year-round, this environment would be ideal for a heat pump water heater, which would operate at high efficiency.

The interior bus maintenance and parking areas have high bay linear fluorescent 4-lamp T5 fixtures. T5 fixtures are obsolete and more difficult to maintain due to a shorter lifespan compared to an LED. LEDs are also significantly more efficient. To replace all of the high bay lights would be considered a capital improvement with possible incentives from Efficiency Vermont. For pricing estimates, the Acuity IBE-LED High Bay fixture (Premium DLC rated) was used.

column2: Main St. Landing Union Station, Wing, and Cornerstone Buildings RCx
Benefit: Benefit Name: Reduce Cooling Towere Pump Minimum Speed
Benefit Narrative: When the throttling valve has been opened 100%, the pressure the pump must overcome will be greatly reduced.  This means the pump will not require as much speed to push the water through the piping and its minimum speed can be reduced.  We recommend programming the pump to modulate speed on the water temperature; increasing speed when water temperature is less than setpoint and reducing speed when it is below setpoint.  A minimum pump speed does not need to be set at the BAS. The VFD should also be checked to ensure it does not have an internal minimum speed setpoint.

column2: Main St. Landing Union Station, Wing, and Cornerstone Buildings RCx
Benefit: Benefit Name: Relocate/ Calibrate BAS Outside Aire Temperaature Sensor
Benefit Narrative: This sensor, which determines the operation of the economizer, is located such that it sees direct sunlight during the middle of the day.  During shoulder seasons, this will falsely turn off the economizer and operate Union Station using the chiller. We recommend TCV check the location and calibration of the sensor.

column2: Main St. Landing Union Station, Wing, and Cornerstone Buildings RCx
Benefit: Benefit Name: Install VFD on the Supply and Return Fans of MAU#2
Benefit Narrative: The make-up air unit for the Cornerstone building has (2) constant speed fans and operates independently of the CO2 levels in the building.  The unit could be placed on the BAS and have VFDs installed on the supply and exhaust fans so that the fans could be ramped down on a daily schedule or on CO2 levels.  This would require a capital investment but would save energy by reducing fan speed (we estimate the fan can operate at an average 75% speed for all annual hours).

column2: Main St. Landing Union Station, Wing, and Cornerstone Buildings RCx
Benefit: Benefit Name: Replace Garage Ligh Bulbs with LED Bulbs
Benefit Narrative: The existing garage fixtures are 80-watt induction type fixtures that were installed in 2012 and are on 24/7.  Upon bulb failure, we recommend replacing them with LED bulbs.  A 65-watt LED can provide the same lumens as the current fixture while producing energy savings.

It appears the boilers operate at a constant 160F supply water temperature, which prevents the boilers from condensing flue gas and results in lower operating efficiency (likely the mid 80% range). By implementing a hot water supply (HWS) temperature reset schedule based on outdoor air temperature, the boilers can be operated as low as a 110F supply water temperature during warm weather. This results in significantly higher combustion efficiency and means that more hours of the year may be able to have 100% of heating hot water provided by refrigeration waste heat.

Believe they could significantly from a recommissioning effort, in which key systems are recommissioned to meet intended design/required operating points with the objective of saving energy, improving comfort, and decreasing operating headaches. HVAC and lighting schedules will also be reviewed, and if opportunities exist to reduce total annual run hours, they will be evaluated. Significant savings can be achieved with modest airflow reduction?a reduction of only 20% fan speed results in fan energy being cut in half. Through rebalancing and recommissioning AHU controls to verify this and other parameters (such as outdoor air fraction) operating costs can be minimized and comfort can be improved.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Refrigeration- Variable Speed CDW Pumping (3 pumps)
Benefit Narrative:
The existing refrigeration condenser water pumps (three nominal 1 ˝ to 2 horsepower), moving a 40% PG antifreeze solution, are traditional in-line constant speed pumps. These pumps have very high annual run hours and consume, in total, about 40,000 kWh per year. Cx Associates noted the pump discharge balance valves had been throttled back to about a 30% open position, suggesting the original pump design head may have been too high.
Given the very high annual pump run hours, changing these pumps out to modern variable speed ?smart pumps? (such as Danfoss Magna 3 series) will provide better temperature control while drastically cutting power use.Cx Associates conducted a limited pump evaluation and determined replacing the existing three constant speed condenser pumps will cut pump energy by about 75%.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Refrigeration - Variable Speed Condenser Fan Motor
Benefit Narrative: The refrigeration dry cooler condensers use multiple constant speed fans, staged sequentially as required to maintain a saturated condenser temperature (SCT) setpoint. This type of condenser fan control is the industry standard, and universally common. On an annual basis, this approach can consume quite a bit of power. But the condenser fans use far more power than necessary because during part load, when only one or two fans are operating, the full surface area of the dry cooler coils fails to be utilized and the individual fans operate at a low efficiency. Condenser fan energy use can be drastically reduced6 to only a fraction of the current staged constant speed fan approach by using variable speed fan motors (either VFD driven or EC motors).

Benefit: Benefit Name: Refrigeration- Heat Recovery/ Head Pressure Control Recommissioning
Benefit Narrative: The refrigeration rack head condensing pressure/temperatures appear to be currently held higher than required year-round to facilitate heat recovery, using a fixed, constant saturated condensing temperature (SCT) setpoints. While recovering beneficial heat, the higher than necessary SCT head pressure setpoint may force the compressors to work harder all year, including just as hard in winter as a hot summer design day. While this does result in more recovered heat, this heat may be significantly more costly to generate (via electricity) than using the high efficiency condensing boilers. Cx Associates conducted an approximate order-of-magnitude study of the energy impact of a fixed SCT compared to floating head pressure control. The fixed SCT setpoint currently in use increases annual refrigeration compressor electrical use by 20%, compared to a floating head setpoint. The refrigeration compressors are the most intense energy consumers in the facility.

column2: UVM On Campus Multipurpose Center Cx
Benefit Narrative: During FPT Cx found that the ERV wheel was being bypassed during normal operation since the controls system was attempting to only allow 6000 CFM through the wheel, which is all it was rated for.  The controls system was looking at the supply air CFM instaed of outside air CFM, so air was being bypassed 100% of the time.

column2: UVM On Campus Multipurpose Center Cx
Benefit Narrative: Static pressure reset was implemented on AHU. It was found that most VAV boxes were typically operating at minimum damper position, so this helped to improve operation as well as save energy.

The existing lighting consists primarily of recessed 3-lamp linear fluorescent troffers with ?Super? T8 (28-watt) lamps and high efficiency electronic ballasts. While compact fluorescent and incandescent lamps have been replaced with LEDs in a couple of areas, we recommend pursuing a comprehensive LED lighting upgrade.    

Along with the benefit of lower wattage compared to fluorescents, most LED fixtures have advanced control capabilities, including options for dimming, integral occupancy sensors, and photocells for daylighting control. Incorporating these controls result in additional energy savings, as well as allowing for adjusting light levels to exactly meet illuminance requirements. LED lamps also have a longer life than fluorescent lamps, leading to maintenance savings on lamp replacements. 

There are several options for LED upgrades, including the following, listed in order from lowest to highest capital cost.  
?	Lamp-only replacements that leave the ballast in place (energy savings are slightly lower for this option).
?	Fixture retrofits with new reflectors that include integral LED lamps and drivers installed in the existing fixture housings.
?	Complete fixture replacements (allow for greatest flexibility and potentially higher energy savings due to whole-system efficiencies).

Cx Associates strongly recommends retrofitting the fixtures at a minimum. BGS has used Philips EvoKit retrofits for other lighting retrofit projects in state buildings, and this, or an equivalent product, could be a good option for the existing linear fluorescent fixtures. 
Savings and costs for this measure are estimated based on assuming one-for-one fixture retrofits. A more detailed lighting design analysis and illumination study may be warranted prior to implementation in order to ensure illuminance targets are met, but not exceeded, and occupant visual comfort is maintained. 

The measure savings estimate includes savings resulting both from vacancy sensors turning off lights when occupants are not present and photocells for daylighting control in perimeter areas. Since many of the lights are already controlled with occupancy sensors, the spaces that already included occupancy sensors were excluded from the vacancy sensor savings calculation.

Based on a review of the electric meter interval data and discussion with facilities staff, we discovered that the building was placed in occupied mode 24/7 from mid-January through April, as well as during July and August. This operation was used order to prevent ?losing? the building (i.e. not being able to maintain temperature setpoints) and to minimize any potential comfort problems and occupant complaints. While it is occasionally necessary to operate building systems continuously during extended periods of extreme weather, normal scheduled operation can resume following the end of the weather event. We recommend implementing programming in the building automation system (BAS) that returns the building to normal schedules after it has been placed in 24/7 occupied mode for a certain number of days (adjustable). This can be used in conjunction with day-ahead weather forecasts to only return to scheduled operation if the forecasted outside air temperature is above a certain minimum (e.g. 0°F) or below a certain maximum (e.g. 85°F). In this way, building operators have the flexibility to manually override HVAC occupancy schedules during extreme weather, but scheduled control will be restored automatically when normal weather conditions return.

While mechanical equipment operates according to schedules during normal building operation, there may be an opportunity for optimizing the occupied schedules to more accurately reflect actual building operating patterns. For example, some of the equipment is scheduled to enter occupied mode as early as 4:30 a.m., although others enter occupied mode as late as 6:00 a.m. In addition, all of the air handling units are scheduled to operate on weekends, even though the museum area, served by MZ-1, is the only space expected to be occupied during weekends.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Install VFDs for Hot Water Pumps
Benefit Narrative:
The existing hot water loop pumps serving the Annex operate at a constant speed regardless of the actual demand at the heating coils being served. By installing variable frequency drives (VFDs) to control the pumps, pump speed could modulate based on flow requirements, resulting in significant power savings during part-load periods. This is due to the exponential relationship between pump motor speed and power based on the pump affinity laws.  As an example, a reduction in speed of just 20% will lead to an approximate 50% power reduction  Since pumps are typically sized to meet the demand during design (worst-case) conditions, the pump could operate at lower speeds for the majority of the heating season. 

In order to take full advantage of the pump speed modulation, most of the three-way valves at air handling unit and VAV box heating coils need to be replaced with two-way valves. Pump speed is then controlled to maintain a differential pressure setpoint using a DP sensor installed toward the end of the loop. The new VFDs would be controlled and monitored by the building automation system.

In order to take full advantage of the pump speed modulation, most of the three-way valves at air handling unit and VAV box heating coils need to be replaced with two-way valves. Pump speed is then controlled to maintain a differential pressure setpoint using a DP sensor installed toward the end of the loop. The new VFDs would be controlled and monitored by the building automation system.

During the summer months when the district steam system is not operating, two propane-fired boilers provide hot water to reheat coils in the Annex. The hot water supply temperature setpoint appears to be 130°F based on an examination of trend data, with typical return water temperatures around 125°F.

The efficiency of condensing boilers is highly dependent on the return water temperature. The boilers operate in condensing mode when the inlet temperature is below around 130°F and efficiency continues to increase as the return water temperature decreases.

Since these boilers serve reheat coils, which typically do not require high water temperatures, it may be possible to reduce the hot water supply temperature setpoint to approximately 120°F. This would result in a lower return water temperature and higher boiler efficiency. Additionally, if measure 4 is implemented (VFDs for hot water pumps), the reduced flow may cause an increase in the temperature differential between the supply and return as pump speed is reduced, resulting in lower return water temperatures.

There were originally several Liebert units in the building to provide cooling for data closets, but most of them have been removed over the years, as equipment in the data closets has moved elsewhere. There is now only one unit remaining in Computer Room 348, which has a glycol-cooled condenser supplied by the glycol loop and a 10-hp pump. We recommend replacing this now oversized pump with a properly sized pump to serve the existing smaller load. Product literature suggests a 1.5 hp pump would be sufficient to serve the one unit, although an engineering study should be performed prior to any replacement to confirm head and flow requirements. Additionally, the Liebert unit is operating to maintain a space temperature setpoint of 72°F with relative humidity setpoint of 45%, environmental conditions which are unnecessarily stringent. ASHRAE recommends that computer rooms be maintained in a temperature range from 59°F to 89°F and relative humidity range from 20% to 80%, so the existing setpoints could be relaxed significantly without harming computer equipment.

The pumping configuration for the centrifugal chiller (CH-1) consists of a primary-only constant speed 15-hp chilled water pump and constant speed 15-hp condenser water pump. We recommend installing a variable frequency drive (VFD) to modulate the chilled water pump speed to meet the building cooling demand, thereby converting the system to variable primary flow. In order to take full advantage of the pump speed modulation, most of the three-way valves at air handling unit cooling coils would need to be replaced with two-way valves. Pump speed would then be controlled to maintain a differential pressure setpoint using a DP sensor installed toward the end of the loop. With a primary-only variable flow configuration, there must be provisions in place to ensure that the minimum required flow is maintained through the chiller whenever it is operating. This is typically accomplished by installing a bypass control valve and flow meter so that even when there is low demand for chilled water at the AHU cooling coils, the system flow rate is maintained above the minimum flow requirement by opening the bypass valve. The new VFD would be controlled and monitored by the building automation system.

The existing lighting consists of a mixture of compact fluorescent lamps (CFL), linear fluorescent fixtures and light emitting diode (LED) lamps. While BGS has been replacing CFLs with LEDs piecemeal as they fail, we recommend pursuing a comprehensive LED lighting upgrade.    

Along with the benefit of lower wattage compared to fluorescents, most LED fixtures have advanced control capabilities, including options for dimming, integral occupancy sensors, and photocells for daylighting control. Incorporating these controls result in additional energy savings, as well as allowing for adjusting light levels to exactly meet illuminance requirements. LED lamps also have a longer life than fluorescent lamps, leading to maintenance savings on lamp replacements. 

There are several options for LED upgrades, including the following listed in order from lowest to highest capital cost: lamp-only replacements that leave the ballast in place, fixture retrofits with new reflectors that include integral LED lamps and drivers installed in the existing fixture housings, and complete fixture replacements. Cx Associates strongly recommends retrofitting the fixtures at a minimum. BGS has used Philips EvoKit retrofits for other lighting retrofit projects in state buildings, and this or an equivalent product could be a good option for the existing linear fluorescent fixtures in the State House. 
Savings and costs for this measure are estimated based on assuming one-for-one fixture retrofits. A more detailed lighting design analysis and illumination study may be warranted prior to implementation in order to ensure illuminance targets are met, but not exceeded, and occupant visual comfort is maintained. 

The measure savings estimate includes savings resulting from vacancy sensors turning off lights when occupants are not present and photocells for daylighting control.

The rooftop snow melt electric resistance heating cable is currently controlled through a manual switch and operates continuously throughout the winter. Often these are left on well into the spring months, since operators forget to turn them off at the end of the season. We recommend integrating the rooftop snowmelt system into the building automation system, and controlling operation based on a seasonal schedule as well as outdoor air temperature. For example, the snowmelt could be automatically enabled from October through April and only operate when the outside air temperature is below 40°F. For additional savings, moisture sensors could be employed to detect when there is actually snow or ice present. Alternatively, control can be integrated with an internet-based weather service to develop routines using forecasted and real-time weather data.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Install Air Curtain at Building Entrance
Benefit Narrative:
Currently the main entrance doors to the building are left open during the summer months to provide a welcoming environment for public tours. This results in a large amount of uncontrolled outside air infiltration and corresponding increased heating and cooling load. An air curtain would provide a high-velocity planar stream of air to create a thermal barrier between indoors and outdoors and minimize air infiltration. These have a typical effectiveness of 60-80%, defined as the amount of energy saved by using the air curtain divided by the amount of energy that would have been used without an air curtain. The effectiveness takes into account the energy penalty due to operating the air curtain fan. By integrating the unit into the BAS, operation can be scheduled and controlled automatically.

Alternatively, a vestibule could be added to the building entrance, which would likely achieve similar energy savings but would have a higher first cost and would require authorization from the Vermont Agency of Commerce and Community Development with regard to historic preservation.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Install VFDs for Hot Water Pumps
Benefit Narrative:
The existing hot water loop pumps operate at a constant speed regardless of the actual demand at the heating coils being served. By installing variable frequency drives to control the pumps, pump speed could modulate based on flow requirements, resulting in significant power savings during part-load periods. This is due to the exponential relationship between pump motor speed and power based on the pump affinity laws, so a reduction in speed of just 20% would lead to approximately a 50% power reduction. Since pumps are typically sized to meet the demand during design (worst-case) conditions, the pump could operate at lower speeds for most of the heating season. 

In order to take full advantage of the pump speed modulation, most of the three-way valves at air handling unit and VAV box heating coils should be replaced with two-way valves. Pump speed is then controlled to maintain a differential pressure setpoint using a DP sensor installed toward the end of the loop. The new VFDs would be controlled and monitored by the building automation system.

Benefit: Benefit Name: ECM Motors for Walk-In Evaporator Fans
Benefit Narrative: The evaporator fans serving the walk-in cooler and freezer in the kitchen run continuously 24/7. These fans not only consume electricity to operate, they also create heat that must be removed by the refrigeration system. We recommend replacing the existing fan motors with brushless DC motors, also known as electronically commutated (ECM) motors. These motors are much more efficient than shaded pole motors, which are the type of motor typically found in this application, leading to savings both in fan motor operation and refrigeration.

While the age and configuration of the air handling units coupled with the need to maintain appropriate environmental conditions for the many historical paintings in the building places a limit on what can realistically be achieved, there may still be opportunities for optimizing the operation of the air handling units. Although we typically recommend ensuring the operating schedules of HVAC systems reflect the actual occupancy patterns of the building in addition to the implementation of unoccupied temperature setbacks, the facilities team is understandably reluctant to implement these measures due to past problems with humidity control and the fact that the building has recently been operating satisfactorily with the existing control sequences. Fortunately, the energy penalty from operating the AHUs continuously 24/7 is somewhat mitigated by the VFDs on the fan motors, which consume very little power at low speed. For example, while on site we observed the AHU-1 supply fan operating at its minimum speed of 50% and consuming 150 watts, while at 100% speed the 5-hp fan would consume around 3,000 watts. 

Some other possibilities for optimizing air handling unit control include the following:

Release BAS Overrides and Tune Setpoints 
While reviewing the existing setpoints and parameters implemented in the building automation system, we noticed a few overrides in place and potential opportunities for slightly adjusting setpoints to reduce energy consumption. Building operators occasionally put in place overrides while troubleshooting a problem or performing maintenance tasks, and sometimes these are left in place after the issue is resolved. Figure 16 shows AHU-4 with a minimum fan speed set to 95%, and the static pressure well above setpoint. For comparison, AHU-5 has a minimum fan speed of 30%. The minimum speed for AHU-4 may have been increased at some point to address a temporary problem and never returned to the original setpoint. 

AHU-1, 2 and 3 are all similar units serving the house and senate chambers. AHU-1 and AHU-3 have a minimum fan speed of 50%, while AHU-2 has a minimum fan speed of 45%. While it would result in minor savings, the minimum fan speed for AHU-2 could be reduced to match the others. Simple tweaks like this may have small impacts individually but can add up cumulatively.
 
Implement Static Pressure Reset
The supply fans for AHU-4, 5 and 6 currently operate to maintain a fixed static pressure at all times. While this static pressure may be necessary during times of peak cooling demand, the setpoint could be lowered during other times. Since fan power is proportional to the cube of the fan speed, even small reductions in fan speed can result in significant fan energy savings. For example, it is common best practice to program the static pressure setpoint to be reset based on VAV box damper position to always maintain one box above 95% open. 

Reduce VAV Box Minimum Flow Rates 
The minimum flow rates for the VAV boxes is currently set to 50% of the design maximum flow rate, which is most likely higher than necessary. Reducing the minimum flow rate would result in fan energy savings and some reheat savings, as well as help to mitigate any overcooling issues. Minimum flow rates can be based on the required minimum ventilation rates for each zone, and these can often be as low as 15% of the design box flow. 

AHU-6 Operation
With a 15-hp supply fan motor, AHU-6 is the largest of the air handling units. It has recently been operating at 100% speed continuously. However, trend data shows that during some periods in the past, the fans reduced speed to 70-80% during unoccupied periods, and the unit was even operating according to a schedule during certain times of the year (Figure 17). There is a large difference in power consumption between a fan operating at 100% speed and one operating at 70%, so determining whether this air handler can indeed modulate to meet space conditions as suggested by historical data would lead to significant savings.

Currently the kitchen exhaust hood fan and associated makeup air unit supply fan run at a constant speed whenever the hood is turned on and operating. Demand control kitchen ventilation (DCKV) involves adding VFDs to the fans to modulate fan speed to reflect the amount of cooking taking place under the hood. Temperature, optical and infrared sensors detect smoke, steam and hot air from the cooking process and adjust fan speeds according to how much exhaust is required. Not only does this result in fan energy savings, but heating energy savings would also be realized due to a lower air volume conditioned by the makeup air unit. Unfortunately, due to the relatively low operating hours of the kitchen and size of the exhaust hood, the investment in a DCKV system does not appear to be warranted given the expected energy savings. DCKV provides the best return on investment in kitchens with exhaust flow rates of 5,000 cfm or higher and long operating hours (at least eight hours per day).

The existing lighting consists primarily of linear fluorescent fixtures with ?Super? T8 (28-watt) lamps and high efficiency electronic ballasts. The main fixture types include 3-lamp 2?x4? recessed troffers and 2-lamp 4? suspended linear pendants. While the existing fluorescent fixtures are relatively efficient, the cost and quality of LED lighting has reached a point that we recommend pursuing a comprehensive LED lighting upgrade to achieve deeper energy savings and realize other benefits as well.    

Along with the benefit of lower wattage compared to fluorescents, most LED fixtures have advanced control capabilities, including options for dimming, integral occupancy sensors, and photocells for daylighting control. Incorporating these controls would result in additional energy savings, as well as allowing for adjusting light levels to exactly meet illuminance requirements. LED lamps also have a longer life than fluorescent lamps, leading to maintenance savings on lamp replacements. 

There are several options for LED upgrades, including the following, listed in order from lowest to highest capital cost and potential energy savings:
?	Lamp-only replacements that leave the ballast in place
?	Fixture retrofits that include integral LED lamps and drivers installed in the existing fixture housings
?	Complete fixture replacements 

Cx Associates strongly recommends retrofitting the fixtures at a minimum. BGS has used Philips EvoKit retrofits for other lighting retrofit projects in state buildings, and this, or an equivalent product, could be a good option for the existing recessed troffers. For the pendants and other fixture types, there are also various retrofit kits available .

Savings and costs for this measure were estimated based on assuming one-for-one fixture retrofits. A more detailed lighting design analysis and illumination study may be warranted prior to implementation in order to ensure illuminance targets are met, but not exceeded, and occupant visual comfort is maintained. 

The measure savings estimate included savings resulting both from vacancy sensors turning off lights when occupants are not present and photocells for daylighting control in perimeter areas. Since many of the lights are already controlled with occupancy sensors, the spaces with existing occupancy sensors were excluded from the vacancy sensor savings calculation.

While the air handling units operate according to schedules implemented in the BAS, there may be an opportunity for optimizing these occupancy schedules to more accurately reflect actual building operating patterns. For example, many of the AHUs are scheduled to operate during the weekend, while the only areas expected to be occupied on the weekend are the BGS Security offices on the third floor. Note that the best schedules are ultimately based on facilities staff?s detailed knowledge of building operation and requirements. The estimated measure savings are based on the proposed schedules listed below and are intended to demonstrate the savings potential of adjusting the schedules. Implementation of this measure requires simple changes to the schedule parameters in the BAS.

Several of the older condensing units serving the split systems are at the end of their useful life and use R-22 refrigerant, which can no longer be produced or imported into the United States as of January 1, 2020. Therefore, these units should be replaced soon regardless of energy savings potential. 

We recommend replacing the units that use R-22 refrigerant with high-efficiency units that meet the CEE  Tier 2 specification. New efficient condensing units typically utilize variable speed scroll compressors to match the capacity to the load, which would lead to substantial cooling energy savings. While this measure is not cost-effective based on energy savings alone, we recommend it as part of a comprehensive upgrade package since the R-22 condensing units are at the end of their useful life and need to be replaced anyway. Additionally, R-22 does significantly more harm to the environment than more current refrigerants and its cost will continue to increase as it is no longer produced

The makeup air unit (MAU) provides 100% outside ventilation air to the third and fourth floors. We recommend replacing this unit with an energy recovery ventilator (ERV) to recover heat from exhaust air that can be transferred to the supply air via a plate heat exchanger. Implementation may require the rerouting of ductwork, so the cost-effectiveness of the measure may ultimately depend on how extensive the reconfiguration needs to be.

There are several refrigerators in kitchen areas and break rooms throughout the facility. Electricity consumption for refrigerators is driven primarily by the size and age of the unit. Older refrigerators can use much more energy than more modern energy efficient units. We recommend replacing the existing refrigerators with units that meet the CEE Tier 3 efficiency specification or are ENERGY STAR rated at a minimum.

Plug loads are estimated to comprise over 25% of the electricity consumption at the building. There are a number of options for effectively managing plug loads , including the following:

1.	Behavioral changes ? Encourage building occupants to power down office equipment at the end of each day. Send e-mail reminders toward the end of each workday to remind staff to turn off computers and monitors and develop a regular habit. 
2.	Install occupancy sensing power strips to detect the presence or absence of a user and automatically turn equipment on or off accordingly.
3.	Enable aggressive power management settings for desktop computers, laptops, monitors and printers. These can often be enacted centrally over an entire network of computers.

The air handling units serving the facility are currently constant volume systems with constant speed supply fans. This measure would convert the units to single zone variable air volume systems by installing variable frequency drives (VFDs) on the supply fans . This will allow the fan speeds to be reduced during low load periods, resulting in substantial fan energy savings. To achieve optimal operation, new condensing units with modulating compressors should be installed so that supply air temperature can be effectively controlled. 

A sample sequence of operation to effectively control single zone VAV systems is shown below:

1.	When unit enters occupied mode, operate supply fan at minimum speed of 50% (adjustable).
2.	If zone temperature is below setpoint, command heating valve to maintain supply air temperature at 90°F. 
3.	If zone temperature is still below setpoint, increase fan speed in order to satisfy zone load. 
4.	If zone temperature is above setpoint, stage DX cooling coil (or economizer if conditions allow) to maintain supply air temperature at 55 °F and operate fan speed at 70%
5.	If zone temperature is still above setpoint with unit at full cooling, increase fan speed in order to satisfy zone load. 

Implementation of this measure requires the installation of VFDs on AHU supply fan motors, adding points for fan speed command and supply air temperature to the BAS, and programming of the automatic control sequence. Due to the relatively small sizes of the motors, implementation of this measure would not be cost-effective at this time. When the units reach the end of useful life, they should be replaced with single zone VAV systems.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Replace Standard Efficiency Motors with Premium Efficiency
Benefit Narrative: Premium efficiency motors are more efficient than motors that are minimally compliant with federal standards. Due to the relatively small motor sizes and low operating hours, early replacement of the motors is not a cost-effective measure. However, premium efficiency motors should be procured when the existing motors fail.

The existing lighting consists primarily of linear fluorescent fixtures with T8 lamps and high performance electronic ballasts (?Super T8s?). We recommend pursuing a comprehensive LED lighting upgrade to retrofit the existing fixtures. Along with the benefit of lower wattage compared to fluorescents, LED lamps also have a longer life than fluorescent lamps, leading to maintenance savings on lamp replacements. 

For the existing linear fluorescent fixtures, we recommend LED retrofit kits to replace the existing ballasts and lamps with new drivers and LED lamps. These retrofit kits often include occupancy sensors as options, which we recommend including for areas such as offices, storage, break rooms, and mechanical rooms.  

Savings and costs for this measure were estimated based on assuming one-for-one fixture retrofits. A more detailed lighting design analysis and illumination study may be warranted prior to implementation in order to ensure illuminance targets are met, but not exceeded, and occupant visual comfort is maintained.

Currently the thermostat in the building maintains the heating space temperature setpoint at 72°F during all hours. Since the building is generally unoccupied overnight from 5 p.m. ? 7 a.m. and on weekends, we recommend implementing an unoccupied heating temperature setback of 64°F  during the hours of 5 p.m. to 6 a.m. This should be fairly straightforward to implement using the existing programmable thermostat.

We investigated a ducted cold climate heat pump system that could be used to provide heating down to outdoor air temperatures of 5°F. This can be used in conjunction with the existing oil-fired furnace, which would operate when temperatures are below 5°F and also at low temperatures when the heat pump has diminished capacity. In addition, this system could provide efficient cooling to replace the existing window AC units. Even if the system is sized to meet the cooling load, we expect that it will be able to meet the majority of the heating requirements as the first stage of heating over the course of the year. 

Savings calculations for this measure include both cooling energy savings, fuel oil heating savings, and increased electric consumption for heating down to 5°F.

Currently the only mechanical ventilation being provided to the building is through the shop exhaust fan, which may only operate intermittently. Given what we know regarding the increased importance of ventilation, especially during COVID-19, we recommend installing an energy recovery ventilator to provide outside ventilation air to building occupants. These units recover energy from exhaust air to pre-condition incoming outside air, heating and adding moisture in the winter, and cooling and dehumidifying in the summer. 

Note that savings for this measure are calculated using a makeup air unit without energy recovery as the baseline, and the measure cost is the incremental cost between a unit with energy recovery and one without. The full project cost to install a mechanical ventilation system for the building would be higher and would include full unit cost and installation, in the range of $5,000 - $10,000.

There is a refrigerator in the break room.  Electricity consumption for refrigerators is driven primarily by the size and age of the unit. Older refrigerators can use much more energy than more modern energy efficient units. When the existing refrigerator fails, we recommend having a procurement plan in place to replace the unit with one that meets the CEE Tier 3 efficiency specification or are ENERGY STAR rated at a minimum.

Due to the relatively small energy savings associated with a new refrigerator, we do not recommend its replacement at this time, but rather encourage purchasing the most energy efficient model available when the equipment reaches its end of useful life.

Air leakage was observed around all of the exterior doors in the building. On doors that are still in good shape, the most cost-effective measure is to check the thresholds, sweeps, and weather-stripping once a year and replace as needed. Even old doors can serve very well if the weather-stripping and sweeps are in good shape. We recommend replacing or adding weather-stripping to the exterior doors at the head, jambs, and especially at the threshold, with commercial grade weather-stripping (www.draftseal.com or Q-lon). We recommend new weather-stripping at the main entrance door (specifically the astragal and sweep), the bulkhead door in the basement, and the rear entrance door.

We recommend insulating the basement walls with 3 inches of closed-cell spray foam, starting at the underside of the floor above to at least 2 feet below grade. In order to reach the top of the wall, the plaster ceiling will need to be removed at the perimeter; however, installing foam above the ceiling will help reduce the amount of heat loss between floors at the rim joist condition. The foam will then need to be covered by either a finished sheetrock layer or a 15-minute spray applied thermal barrier for fire protection. 

We acknowledge that there is some risk insulating a basement that is in a flood zone. There was some evidence of past water damage to the brick columns, though we are unsure if this is from flooding or capillary action through the slab. Current conditions, though, indicate that the basement is dry.  We did not see any evidence of recent water entry or damp building materials that require remediation. We recommend using closed-cell spray foam as an insulation material because it is moisture resistant and would survive flooding without damage. However, in the event of a flood, the spray foam would reduce the drying potential of any wood or stone that it is in contact with.  It is impossible to predict how long it would take for all building materials to dry out after a flood, but it would take longer if the foundation wall was coated in closed-cell spray foam.         

Stopping the insulation 2 feet below grade increases the drying potential and reduces the risk that water in the soil outside the foundation will freeze and push the foundation wall in, commonly described as frost action.  Since the lower part of the foundation wall will remain uninsulated, it can continue to conduct some heat energy into the soil to keep it from freezing and avoid frost action.  In addition, research has shown that frost action on foundation walls only occurs if the basement is unheated. See this link for more information about insulating stone foundations: https://www.buildingscience.com/documents/insights/bsi-045-double-rubble-toil-trouble.

In addition to the above, we do think it is a good idea to implement a sacrificial cementitious parge coat over the brick columns, which appear to be structural. This will reduce the efflorescence and spalling of the brick itself at the base. Upkeep with the parge coat as it deteriorates will be critical in maintaining the brick?s current condition.

The attic mansard slopes and high flat roof have metal on the exterior (presumably uninsulated on the exterior of sheathing) but are insulated on the interior side of the sheathing with foil-face fiberglass batts. However, there is no air barrier and currently warm air from the mansard wall cavity (conditioned air) in the floor below is able to travel into the attic slope cavity and get behind the insulation, which nullifies the effectiveness of the insulation. 

Additionally, the framing and finish at the perimeter of the flat roof cornices is quite open. Daylight could be seen and, in pulling back a batt of insulation, air could easily be felt pouring into the attic space. With the batt in place, air movement is diffused somewhat, but fiberglass is still an air permeable insulation and the foil face is not perfectly tight to the wood framing. The roof hatch also appears to be original and makes a very poor seal to its curb. 

Assuming an interior closed-cell spray foam approach is employed for the mansard walls in level 3, then we suggest maintaining the same strategy across the attic mansard walls and to the attic flat roof, removing existing insulation first. Insulate to the full framing depth or about 5.5?, whichever comes first. 

The attic flat roof could be addressed similar to the low flat roofs (described below); rubber roof membrane, exterior insulation, and a self-adhered air/vapor barrier over decking. This approach also has the advantage of spanning over the elevator shaft, which avoids any complicated air/thermal boundary conditions. Again, the interface between attic mansard walls and this flat roof will need to be detailed carefully for air and thermal barrier continuity, and the addition of exterior insulation will change the profile of the roof. However, adding exterior insulation to both the low and high flat roofs should keep the general profile of the roof proportionally the same. While the impact of this measure will be less significant, since the attic is already insulated, there is value in creating a continuous air barrier from the walls and roof below to the attic.

The mansard walls and roof have a metal exterior finish and no insulation on the interior side of the sheathing. We are unsure what the layers are beneath the metal, but it is unlikely, due to its age and profile thickness, that there is any insulation on the exterior side of the sheathing. However, because the metal will act as a vapor barrier on the exterior side, and without knowing the layers in between, it is difficult to anticipate the level of risk adding an air and thermal barrier will have. Therefore, we are unable to definitively recommend any one particular approach for insulating from the interior side. We first recommend removing a small section of the mansard and low flat roofs as samples in order to confirm all of the layers of these systems. This will help better inform retrofit options. Investigative demolition like this is usually a process that occurs in the early design phases of a major renovation.

Due to the age, condition, and numerous patches/repairs, we can say it is time to replace it. A number of areas have been patched with a rubber roof membrane and the metal appears to be warped and rusting, which are signs of deterioration. In consideration of a full replacement, the strategies that should be getting consideration are a conventional rubber roof membrane system with exterior insulation and self-adhered air/vapor barrier (flat low roofs), and metal roof replacement and underlayment with closed-cell spray foam between wood framing (steep mansard walls/roof). The former strategy for low slope roofs is tried and true and the latter approach at the steep mansard walls will integrate with interior spray foam on the mass walls (which we think is the most likely option) in the most monolithic way. Target R-30 or greater in the flat roof and fill mansard walls to the full framing depth or 5.5?, whichever comes first. 

The designer will need to pay careful attention to the transition detailing of these two roof systems in order to ensure air and thermal barriers are integrated. The air and thermal barriers will be making a transition from the interior of the envelope assembly to the exterior. It is feasible to accomplish, but needs deliberate attention or else it could be missed. Adding exterior insulation to the low flat roofs will change the profile of the roof and the appearance with respect to the higher roof, so alternatively closed-cell spray foam could be used in the joist bays here instead, pending confirmation of materials from roof sampling.

Cost approximations assume the above general strategies are employed for the mansard and lower level roofs, and currently assume zero insulation in the existing assembly, based on evidence to date. 

These roofs are in need of replacement so regardless of actual return on investment, improving the air and thermal barriers is only going to be most cost-effective concurrent with roof replacement.

Mass masonry buildings, like this one, were historically built without the typical air, vapor, water, and thermal control layers that are found in modern day construction. The walls are constructed using only brick and mortar that is layered in multiple rows to form a solid mass wall. The primary method for preventing water intrusion in mass masonry buildings, after bulk water management practices that keep water away from brick, is to rely on the brick to serve as a rain-screen and hygric buffer. Any moisture absorbed into the brick is stored until it can slowly dry out as moisture evaporates towards either the interior side or the exterior side of the wall. Knowing that the masonry façade will not be totally free of bulk water exposure, and will therefore hold moisture, careful consideration has to be made when proposing new control layers so as not to compromise the drying capacity of the mass wall. For example, installing too much vapor-closed R-value on the interior of masonry reduces inward drying capacity as well as available heat to get to the brick to drive moisture outward. The risk is freeze-thaw cycling which can spall bricks on a decades old building that has never had such an issue before. 

If possible, the approach with the least amount of risk would be to add an Exterior Insulation Finishing System (or EIFS) to the exterior side of the building. This would limit the amount of water that is absorbed by the brick and still allow for drying to the interior side. In this case, we recommend a drained EIFS system over an undrained system, since the drained system over time has proven to be structurally more resilient. A drained EIFS system typically consists of multiple layers. The first layer is an air and water barrier that is applied directly onto the brick exterior. This should be a vapor permeable product to allow vapor diffusion through the brick to the exterior if need be. After that, an adhesive is applied with a notched trowel. The notches create the space needed for water to drain behind the rest of the system. Next, a layer of rigid insulation board is applied to the adhesive and can sometimes be mechanically fastened to the brick. Finally, a stucco and mesh finish coat is applied over the insulation to give the system its durability and aesthetic. We suggest a 4? EIFS system, although an important contingency to this approach is whether or not this building will be overseen by a historical preservation committee, in which case this strategy will not be possible.

Given that an exterior solution is often not ideal for an historic building such as this, interior framing of exterior walls could be gutted and insulation and an air barrier added to the interior side of the wall. In this scenario, we recommend closed-cell spray foam be applied against the brick. This is the most straightforward interior option as it can serve as both the air and thermal barrier and is also the most vetted solution. Regarding the freeze-thaw cycling mentioned above, 2? of closed-cell spray foam is generally considered safe, but a WUFI analysis would be recommended in order to determine if these walls could safely manage additional R-value. Spray foam on the interior may have the advantage of integrating more seamlessly with mansard roof retrofits from an air and thermal barrier continuity perspective.  

Pending WUFI results, we recommend adding at least R-20 to meet current energy codes. 

We do not recommend a retrofit option like blown-in cellulose or fiberglass. Blowing insulation into the wall cavity, especially one only 2 inches in depth, will not do a sufficient job at preventing convective air movement in the cavity, and therefore creates a significant risk of condensation forming. Additionally, neither of these materials would perform well in the event they were wetted.

Out of anticipation of the most likely approach, our approximate costs and payback assume an interior gut renovation with closed-cell spray foam.

Most of the windows in the building are wood frame, single-hung, single-pane windows with weighted pulleys and an exterior storm. The wood frame windows, after so many years of wear and tear, do not create a tight air seal when closed, and the single pane of glass offers very little thermal resistance. They are also difficult to open and close, and many of the windows have faulty latches. In theory, the exterior storm windows should help improve both the air leakage and thermal resistance of the wood frame windows, but significant gaps around the perimeter of the storm windows still allow air to move around them. Additionally, much of the exterior paint, which in all likelihood is old lead-based paint, at the exterior windowsills and jamb extensions is heavily flaking off the surface. Where steel is present and exposed beneath, rust is present. Perimeter sealants at the window rough openings is beyond its useful life as well. At foundation-level window infills, the wood frames are beginning to rot.   

The window units themselves could be repaired and refurbished, but the air leakage will only improve slightly and there would still be a fair amount of cost involved in ensuring smooth and durable operability. Additionally, the surrounding concerns in major need of attention would go unaddressed. We have collected data from a project where two historical windows, similar to the ones tested at 110 State Street, were tested before and after they were refurbished/retrofitted. The retrofit included metal weather stripping and new ropes/pulleys. The data showed a 48% decrease in air leakage after the retrofit was complete

Estimated Window Energy and Cost Savings if Windows are Refurbished:

Even with a 48% decrease in air leakage per window, the older wood windows would still be 25 times as leaky as modern day windows. BVH used the data recorded on site to calculate an average air leakage rate of 11.3 CFM natural per window. From the floor plans given, we were able determine that there are a total of 17 larger windows and 8 smaller windows. Based on this information, we calculated an estimated savings of 24.4 MMBTU per year if the windows were refurbished. Because of these relatively low savings, we would recommend that the windows are instead replaced with modern windows. (See Appendix C for recommendations on new windows.) Not only would the air leakage be drastically reduced, but the R-value of the windows can be doubled, further increasing the savings. There are other benefits to replacing the windows besides the cost and energy savings. One benefit is ease of use; again, many of the windows are difficult/impossible to open. 

Estimated Window Energy and Cost Savings if Windows are Replaced:

If the windows are replaced with R-4 windows, the savings are greatly increased. We calculated the existing average R-value is R-2. New windows should have a leakage no greater than 0.1 CFM/SF@75 Pascals. From the data recorded, the decrease in air leakage if the windows are replaced is 99%. We used the average surface area for the windows tested while on site and multiplied it by the number of windows each building has to get the estimated surface area of all windows. Using these data, the estimated savings from replacing the windows was calculated to amount to an estimated savings of 92.2 MMBTU per year. This is nearly 4 times as much savings per year than if the windows were refurbished. It should be noted that we can only estimate the decrease in air leakage through the windows themselves. Replacing the windows would also improve their seal to the building/rough opening, decreasing the building air leakage even further.

While reviewing the BAS graphics, we noticed a number of potential opportunities we would classify as retrocommissioning measures, which are low-cost measures involving the adjustment of schedules, setpoints, and control sequences, as opposed to more capital intensive measures such as equipment replacement.

The domestic hot water heater in the basement is a 40-gallon electric resistance water heater. Heat pump water heaters are much more efficient than electric resistance water heaters, and help dehumidify the surrounding space, which may negate the need for operating a dehumidifier in the summer months. For the type of use in this building, the unit could operate in heat pump only mode, which has a slower recovery time but is three times more efficient than an electric water heat. Hybrid mode uses the heat pump for most hours but will also use an electric resistance element to achieve faster recovery times if required. We recommend procuring a heat pump water heater that exceeds the minimum ENERGY STAR requirements.

The existing lighting in office areas consists of a mix of surface mounted linear indirect T8 fixtures with ?Super? T8 (28-watt) lamps and high efficiency electronic ballasts and suspended chandeliers with CFL bulbs. Basement areas have 3-lamp linear fluorescent 2?x4? recessed troffers. Corridors have T8 lighting and compact fluorescent (CFL) pendants and sconces. While T8 fixtures have been replaced with LEDs in a couple of basement areas, we recommend pursuing a comprehensive LED lighting upgrade.   
 
Along with the benefit of lower wattage compared to fluorescents we recommend including advanced lighting controls, including occupancy sensors, and photocells for daylighting control. Incorporating these controls result in additional energy savings, as well as allowing for adjusting light levels to exactly meet illuminance requirements. LED lamps also have a longer life than fluorescent lamps, leading to maintenance savings on lamp replacements. 

There are several options for LED upgrades, including the following, listed in order from lowest to highest capital cost:

?	Fixture retrofits with new reflectors that include integral LED lamps and drivers installed in the existing fixture housings
?	Complete fixture replacements 

Cx Associates strongly recommends retrofitting the lamps at a minimum. BGS has used Philips EvoKit retrofits for other lighting retrofit projects in state buildings, and this, or an equivalent product, could be a good option for the existing linear fluorescent fixtures. Although these retrofit kits offer more streamlined controls, they did not prove cost-effective in this building. Instead, the measure savings calculations assume a LED lamp-only replacement.  Although these consume slightly more energy than a retrofit kit, they also have a significantly lower cost.

Savings and costs for this measure were estimated based on assuming one-for-one lamp retrofits. A more detailed lighting design analysis and illumination study may be warranted prior to implementation to ensure illuminance targets are met, but not exceeded, and occupant visual comfort is maintained. 

The measure savings estimate includes savings resulting both from vacancy sensors turning off lights when occupants are not present and photocells for daylighting control in perimeter areas.

116 State Street is not currently equipped with a central air conditioning system; space cooling needs are met using a total of ten (10) window air conditioners, with a total capacity of approximately 100,000 Btu/h, spread throughout the building. These window air conditioners are of various vintage and efficiencies, and overall do not serve to fully satisfy all spaces in the building.  These units are left in place year-round.

We propose installing a multi-zone variable refrigerant flow (VRF) system equipped with heat recovery to satisfy the space cooling loads in the building. A system like this offers an increase in efficiency for space cooling as well as the ability to provide shoulder season heating with an improved coefficient of performance if equipped to operate as a heat pump. This system is also desirable as it will reduce the heat loss in the spaces that occurs from the increased leakage and infiltration caused by leaving the window air conditioning units in year-round.

The alternative central cooling system design would consist of an air handler with a direct expansion coil.  Given the age and construction of the building, installation of such a central air conditioner would be difficult.  The multiple styles of VRF terminal equipment and relatively non-invasive installation of refrigerant piping and condensate disposal lines would offer a simpler solution to bring cooling to much of the building.

Savings calculations for this measure include both cooling energy savings, heating steam savings and increased electric consumption for heating.

Although this measure does not provide large savings in our calculations, and therefore cannot recommend pursuing this measure solely from an economic perspective, we feel the reduced steam consumption due to window AC units left installed in the winter, and the increased occupant comfort by cooling more spaces and reducing winter drafts, are worthy of consideration when determining if this measure should be implemented.

While most of the mechanical equipment operates according to schedules during normal building operation, there appears to have been a long-standing occupancy override in the building automation system for the heat recovery unit (HRU-1). HRU-1 provides 100% outside air to the building and utilizes a heat recovery wheel, DX coil, and HW coil to temper the supply air to the spaces.

During the investigation of the trend data, the occupancy override appears to have been in place since at least August 2019. This is causing the fans, heat recovery wheel, hot water coil, and condensing unit to operate many more hours and consume more energy than necessary. Returning HRU-1 to its normal occupancy schedule will allow the unit to provide ventilation air for the building only when there are occupants in the building.

The building has a hydronic snowmelt control system that serves the front steps area. The snowmelt system utilizes the district steam and a heat exchanger to transfer heat to the glycol protected piping that serves the snowmelt zone. It was understood from the building operator interview that the front steps and walkway immediately adjacent are the only snowmelt areas.

During investigation of the snowmelt control system, it was noted that the system is not equipped with a snow/ice melt sensor and does not have any control for ?idle? or ?snowmelt? operations which are typically found in modern snowmelt systems. The current control utilizes an outdoor air reset and outdoor air enable. The current control allows steam enable at 40?F and heat (glycol hot water) enable at 32?F. The glycol hot water return temperature setpoint is reset between 110?F and 135?F for outside air temperatures between 45?F and 0?F. 

Operating the snowmelt system in this manner results in nearly continuous hot water supply to the slab and stairs during the winter. With an approximate energy consumption of 250 Btu/hr*sqft , the energy consumption for this system was approximately 281.6 MMBtu for October 2019 through May 2020.

We propose installing a packaged snowmelt control system that utilizes an outdoor air temperatures sensor, in-slab temperature sensor and an aerial snow sensor to control the snowmelt system. This offers the ability to enable the snowmelt system only when snowfall is detected and will ?idle? the slab at a near freezing condition when outdoor air temperatures indicate freezing conditions. During all other temperatures, the snowmelt system will be off. The snowmelt control system will have the ability to control the pump and/or steam control valve, and the controls can be integrated into the BAS for adjustment of key operating parameters.

All this being said, BGS engineers have expressed that the existing system has been tweaked over the years to the point where it operates effectively at removing snow and preventing water from getting under the stone steps, which would cause the steps to move out of alignment. We would defer to the expertise of the facilities team on these types of issues and would not recommend something that may result in structural damage to the steps.

There is at least one full-size refrigerator in the building. This refrigerator appears to be an older vintage and because of that there may be an opportunity to reduce energy consumption with a newer refrigerator. Newer, ENERGY STAR refrigerators use less energy than older models. Although this measure offers a small total energy savings, the cost of the measure is also minimal, and incentives may be available from Efficiency Vermont for the purchase of an energy-efficient refrigerator.

Due to the relatively small energy savings associated with a new refrigerator, we do not recommend its replacement at this time, but rather consider purchasing the most energy efficient model available when the equipment reaches its end of useful life.

During investigation of the building automation system, it was noticed that the heating hot water pumps P-3 and P-4 were commanded off, but their status was on. This is unusual unless the pumps have been left in ?Hand? mode however the pumps were set at 0% according to the output from the VFD. 

Further investigation of the trend data (specifically the differential pressure) indicated that although the pumps appear to be in ?Hand? mode, they appear to be operating at the occupied 5 PSI setpoint and not 24/7. The trend data further showed that there are many hours outside the heating season that the hot water pumps operate. It is unclear from the occupancy schedule why the pumps would be operating during these times when district steam is not available.

We suggest the root cause of this unexpected operation be investigated further. This investigation into the BAS logic and VFD setpoints will likely need to be performed by the controls vendor or someone who has BAS access. The unwanted operational hours came to a total of 1,160 hours for May 2020 through July 2020 and August 2019 through October 2019 or 17% of the total pump energy use for these pumps.

A survey of all the spaces in the building and use of the ASHRAE Standard 62.1 indicates the design outdoor airflow rate for the heat recovery unit (HRU-1) is somewhat oversized for the space ventilation requirements. HRU-1 is designed to provide 1,650 CFM of supply air. According to our survey of the spaces and applying the ventilation rates described in 62.1, the spaces could use as little as 1,313 CFM supplied outside air. Rebalancing the airflow rates will require a Testing, Balancing and Adjusting (TAB) contractor to perform room by room re-balancing. A more detailed calculation of the ASHRAE 62.1 ventilation requirements may be warranted as our assumption of space use may differ from how they are truly being utilized.

This measure offers saving through the reduction in fan energy use.  It will also provide a small reduction in both hot water and DX coil energy use as the reduction in airflow reduces the amount of energy required to temper the air to the appropriate conditions. 

Due to the high cost of the rebalancing efforts from the TAB contractor and the minimal savings, we are not recommending this measure.  Additionally, with regard to the COVID pandemic, higher ventilation rates are advantageous to virus mitigation and the State may not want to reduce these rates at this time.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Replace motors with Premium Efficiency Motors
Benefit Narrative:
Overall energy consumed by the fans and motors represents the largest electric energy use in the HVAC system for 116 State Street. Energy consumption could be reduced using premium efficiency motors for all fans and pump motors. Replacement motors could be utilized for the following equipment:
?	P-1 / P-2 ? Glycol Hot Water Pumps (1/2 HP)
?	P-3 / P-4 ? Heating Hot Water Pumps (3/4 HP)
?	HRU-1 ? Supply and Exhaust Fans (3HP Supply Fan, 2 HP Exhaust Fan)

The HRU-1 fan motor efficiency was unable to be confirmed, however the glycol hot water pumps (P-1/P-2) motor efficiency were confirmed and found to be 62% while the heating hot water pumps (P-3/P-4) motor efficiency were confirmed and found to be 75%. As a comparison, the NEMA Premium Efficiency standard is 89.5% for the 3 HP motor, 86.5% for the 2 HP motor, 85.5% for the 1HP  and ľ HP motors, and does not apply to motors less than ľHP. The ˝ HP motors for P-1/P-2 could be replaced with 78% efficient motors and P-3/P-4 could be replaced with 85.5% efficient motors, HRU-1 supply fan motor could be replaced with an 89.5% efficient motor, and HRU-1 exhaust fan motor could be replaced with an 86.5% efficient motor.

However, due to the marginal increase in efficiency and the motor replacement cost, this measure is not recommended until the current motors fail. When the motors are at end-of-life and replacement motors must be ordered, it should be ensured that premium efficiency motors are installed. If there are backup motors that are not yet installed, their efficiency should be checked and if they do not meet the premium efficiency rating described above, we recommend they be returned (if possible) and replaced with premium efficiency motors.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Smart Pump for Hot Water Systems
Benefit Narrative:
Currently the building is equipped with two pairs of heating hot water pumps, one which serves hot water radiators throughout the building as well as a glycol hot water system which serves a heating coil in HRU-1. The existing pumps operate in a lead-standby configuration where only one pump operates at a time. The current heating hot water pumps are sized for 40 GPM at 32? of head and are equipped with 3/4HP motors, and the glycol hot water pumps are sized for 32 GPM at 19? of head and are equipped with a ˝ HP motor. The heating hot water pumps operate at variable speed and are controlled to maintain a differential pressure setpoint of 5 psi, while the glycol hot water pumps are constant speed.

Modern pumps are coming equipped with ECM motors, ?smart? controls to vary the flow, and increased pump efficiency. Utilizing a new pump offers savings based on pump and motor efficiency alone.
