At lower outside air temperatures, the compressor will not have to work as hard to remove heat from the walk-in coolers and freezers. Note that new condensing units often come with floating head pressure control as an option so if new units are installed, a controls retrofit would not be required. 
ECM Motors for Evaporator Fans
Evaporator fans serving walk-in coolers and freezers typically run continuously, 24/7. These fans not only consume electricity to operate, they also create heat that must be removed by the refrigeration system. There are two common ways that energy use by evaporator fans can be reduced. The first is to replace traditional shaded-pole motors with higher efficiency brushless DC motors, also known as electronically commutated (ECM) motors. The second method is to control the fan speed. Evaporator fans run continuously, though the refrigeration circuit is only operating part of the time. The rest of the time, the evaporator fans are just circulating air. Evaporator fan speed controllers allow fans to operate at low speed when the refrigeration circuit is off, saving additional energy. 

The walk-in coolers and freezers have older and less efficient evaporator fan units. However, the manufacturer (Bally) reported that EC motors cannot be retrofitted into the existing unit because the models have been discontinued. Therefore, in order to upgrade the equipment, the evaporator fan sections would need to be replaced. This project would have a relatively long payback when only considering energy savings. However, given that the evaporators are older and the models have been discontinued, it may still be worthwhile to replace and upgrade with evaporator units that have ECM motors and 2-speed control.
Reach-in Refrigerators and Ice Makers 
Other refrigeration equipment in the kitchen includes reach-in refrigerators and ice makers. While it would not be cost-effective to replace these at this time based on energy savings alone, ENERGY STAR models should be procured when this equipment reaches end of life.

Since the facility electric meter is located on the primary side of the building transformers, the transformer losses directly impact the metered electric consumption. While transformers are very efficient devices, since they are always operating, even a small increase in efficiency can yield significant savings. Since the facility was originally constructed, there have been new federal requirements put in place effective January 2016 for transformer efficiency, which are similar to the NEMA premium efficiency category . Replacing the existing transformers with new efficient transformers would reduce losses and result in demand and energy savings. While this measure does not appear to be cost-effective at this time, a strategy for procuring premium efficiency transformers should be put in place for when the existing transformers reach the end of useful life.

Benefit: Benefit Name: 'Smart' Pump for Heating Hot Water System
Benefit Narrative:
Currently the core facilities building is equipped with a heating hot water pump which serves hot water reheat coils throughout the facility. The existing pumps operate in a lead-standby configuration where only one pump operates at a time. The current pumps are sized for 100 GPM at 50? of head and are equipped with 3HP motors. The pumps operate at variable speed and are controlled to maintain a differential pressure setpoint of 10 psi. 

Additionally, there are 16 domestic hot water circulation pumps throughout the campus. These pumps are small, cartridge, fractional horsepower pumps, similar to Grundfos UPS 15-55. The pumps are controlled to maintain a specific return water temperature. This control arrangement is very similar to the options that ?smart? pumps offer, so therefore we did not consider installing ?smart? domestic hot water circulation pumps to be an effective energy conservation measure.

Modern pumps are coming equipped with ECM motors, ?smart? controls and increased pump efficiency. Utilizing a new pump offers savings based on pump and motor efficiency alone. A ?smart? pump equipped with integral controls would allow it to reset the pressure setpoint and therefore pump speed based on the system?s response to heating demand in the building. The savings for a new pump were calculated based on increased pump efficiency alone and do not include any savings for ?smart? pump controls because current pumping is utilizing a differential pressure control with a variable frequency drive and additional savings from the ?smart? pump features will be relatively minimal. We don?t believe it is worthwhile to replace the current pump with a similarly sized ?smart? pump based on the pumping configuration currently in place.

Our savings and costs calculations assume a Grundfos TPE3 50-200. This pump selection was based on the original mechanical schedules flow and head requirements. It is possible that a further investigation into the pumping requirements might show that there is an opportunity to downsize the heating hot water pump, which would result in larger savings than presented here.

Currently, there is one electric meter serving the entire campus of seven buildings. Installing electric submeters for each building would allow for more granular data collection and analysis.  Benchmarking could be performed for each individual building, allowing for more effective energy management and tracking of savings from energy efficiency projects. The submeters can be integrated into the building automation system for ease of monitoring.

Installing steam meters in various locations can yield insight into the operation of the steam system and help direct maintenance activities. For example, a meter located where the steam leaves the energy building can allow for the calculation of the boiler fuel-to-steam efficiency. Similarly, meters located at each building can indicate the efficiency of the steam distribution system. As baselines are established for these metrics, sudden changes or drifts over time can be identified and guide maintenance staff on where to direct attention.

Steam traps can have a failure rate of 15-20% annually, resulting in increased energy consumption if failed open as the trap constantly passes steam. We recommend conducting a survey of all steam traps in the facility and implementing an annual steam trap testing and repair program.

During the energy audit, facilities staff indicated that the mechanical systems would be undergoing testing, adjusting, and balancing (TAB) sometime in the next year. We strongly recommend that this be done, as properly balanced systems can result in reduced energy consumption and increased environmental air quality. For example, the design flow for the large 100% outdoor air units serving the inmate housing areas should be verified ? too high of a flow results in energy waste, while too low could compromise indoor air quality. Minimum outdoor airflow and corresponding outside air damper positions should be verified for all air handling units.

We did not examine all of the condensing coils for refrigeration equipment and facility staff may already be including this in their preventive maintenance activities. We recommend cleaning coils quarterly and ensuring there is sufficient clearance for adequate airflow.

The building is equipped with a condensate meter to measure steam consumption, but historical data was not available. We recommend repairing any issues that are preventing the metering and collection of this data. Accurate metering can help establish a baseline and allow for tracking progress toward energy goals over time, along with the identification of any anomalies that may indicate issues with building operation.

The building currently uses pneumatic actuators to control valves and dampers, which has air supplied by a compressor located in the basement. Electropneumatic transducers are used to translate the control signal from the BAS to the proper air pressure. Replacing the pneumatic controls with direct digital controls (DDC) would allow for the implementation of best-in-class control sequences which require feedback on valve and damper positions, and also allow for the decommissioning of the air compressor that is currently providing compressed air to the system. This would be a significant capital project and would most likely not be cost effective from purely an energy savings and maintenance savings standpoint. However, it may make sense to include as part of a comprehensive whole-building retrofit project.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Ducted Outdoor Air and Return Air
Benefit Narrative: Currently the attic serves as a return plenum for AHU-2 and AHU-3. Air is returned from the spaces served by these units directly into the attic, and is drawn into the AHUs along with some amount of outdoor air that enters through the louvers on the walls . We recommend ducting the return air and outside air to the AHUs, which would most likely result in reduced energy consumption and improved indoor air quality. By installing modulating outside air and return dampers for the AHUs, the introduction of outdoor air can be better controlled and an economizer control sequence can be implemented for free cooling when outdoor air conditions are appropriate.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Ensure Building Energy Meters are Calibrated and Operating Properly
Benefit Narrative: There are issues with the building-level condensate meter resulting in gaps in data, causing evaluators to rely on estimations for whole-building energy consumption.  To establish an effective energy management program, it is essential that energy meters are calibrated and operating properly. For additional granularity, electric sub-meters can be installed to meter end uses such as plug loads, lighting, and HVAC.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Ensure Building Energy Meters are Calibrated and Operating Properly
Benefit Narrative: There were issues with the building-level electric and condensate meters resulting in gaps in data and estimates for whole-building energy consumption.  To establish an effective energy management program, it is essential that energy meters are calibrated and operating properly. For additional granularity, electric sub-meters can be installed to meter end uses such as plug loads, lighting, and HVAC.

We spot checked a couple of steam traps while on site, and they appeared to be operating properly. Since steam traps typically have a relatively high failure rate, we recommend conducting a survey of all steam traps in the facility and implementing an annual steam trap testing and repair program. This can be part of a campus wide O&M effort to achieve a high performance district steam system. Previously, an ESCO had been periodically replacing traps as part of a preventive maintenance program, but this was discontinued at some point. We encourage BGS to restart a similar program, either in-house or contracted to a vendor.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Engage Occupants Regarding Electric Space Heaters
Benefit Narrative: We observed a number of electric space heaters in many of the offices, and electric meter data showed significantly higher consumption during the winter months. Occupant thermal comfort is the most important service a building provides, but we encourage BGS to engage with building occupants to see if there are ways to minimize electric space heater use.

the building is equipped with an electric sub-meter and condensate meter, but historical data was not available. We recommend repairing any issues that are preventing the metering and collection of this data. Accurate metering can help establish a baseline and allow for tracking progress toward energy goals over time, along with the identification of any anomalies that may indicate issues with building operation.

column2: Gifford Health Care Chiller RCx and Capacity Analysis
Benefit Narrative: The chillers provided a constant 43°F supply water temperature. While this may be necessary during days with high ambient temperature and humidity, this temperature is unnecessarily low when temps and especially humidity are lower. By increasing the CHW temperature strategically, energy savings of 1-2% per degree can be achieved when outdoor conditions are favorable

column2: Gifford Health Care Chiller RCx and Capacity Analysis
Benefit Narrative:
The 15-hp chilled water secondary pumps (P-7 and P-8), while served by VFDs, do not significantly vary speed throughout the year. See Figure 3. This indicates that while the hardware is in place in the central plant to provide variable speed pumping, much of the terminal equipment is still piped as constant flow?in other words, a three port valve shifts flow between the coil, or the return port. Further, the setpoint for the secondary pumps appears to be a constant 40 PSI differential.
A fraction of the terminal equipment can be converted to 2-port control valves. A differential pressure reset scheme can be implemented for the secondary CHW loop. The best way to achieve this is for the BAS to poll all terminal equipment CHW valves and reset pump DP setpoint accordingly. As the most critical CHW valve opens more than 90%, the DP starts to be reset upwards. As the worst-case valve drops below 80% open, the DP is reset downwards. In this manner, the lowest operating DP can be achieved for any operating condition.

column2: Gifford Health Care Chiller RCx and Capacity Analysis
Benefit Narrative: During the shoulder and winter months, there are many hours when the chiller is operating, but outdoor air conditions are favorable for free cooling by a dry cooler?basically a radiator, like the one in place for GMC?s emergency generator. We plotted the cooling load during periods when the outdoor air temperature was 45°F or below and found that a significant portion of the year could be served periodically by a 30-ton dry cooler.. This would act as an additional chiller and would generate free cooling at a fraction of the power associated with DX cooling.

The current return water setpoint is 85°F. We recommend implementing a reset schedule that adjusts this setpoint to the minimum condenser water temperature required by the chillers based on outside air wetbulb temperature. This will increase the run time of the cooling tower, but this will be more than offset by the increase in efficiency of the chiller.

Currently the cooling tower fan sequence operates 1 cell with 1 fan and no spray pump in a lead/lag fashion. In winter, maintenance staff need to manually chop ice and sweep snow off before they start the fan due to ice buildup. The control sequence could be modified to operate both cells simultaneously and enable the existing VFDs to operate on differential temperature. This would keep fans off longer due to the increased area available for heat rejection, while also operating the fans at considerably lower speeds. Two motors operating in parallel at low speeds consume less energy than one motor at high speed.Although not an energy saving measure, while the cooling tower sequence is being modified, we recommend either programming a de-icing sequence or adding an alarm to alert facilities before the fans can be started when the outside air temperature is below 35°F, a time when there could be ice built up on top of the fans.

The chilled water supply temperature setpoint is 45°F, but Dan Rich stated that the building can handle a setpoint of 50°F for all times except for some very high temp/humidity days. We suggest programming the BAS to operate the chillers with a reset schedule that modulates the chilled water supply temperature setpoint between 45°F and 50°F depending on the outside air enthalpy

None of the building air handlers were observed to have a programmed supply air temperature sequence. SAT setpoints minimize energy use and relieve a burden offf of facility staff. Aimplementing a supplu air temperatrue reset reduced both AHU cooling and VAV reheat energy. Savings are difficult to estimate. Action: implement a supplu air temperature reset sequence that is based on teminal loads per ASHRAE Guideline 36

column2: UVMMC Secondary CHWS Balancing Engineering Services
Benefit Narrative: AHU 1 through 3 are air handlers with terminal VAV systems and utilize a static pressure setpoint to maintain airflow at VAVs. Implementing a static pressure setpoint will decrease fan speed and fan energy consumption by allowing the static setpoint to be lowered during low load times. Action: Implement a static pressure reset sequence per ASHRAE Guideline 36 which uses VAV damper position to adjust the air handler reset static pressure point

column2: Unilever Seventh Generation Renovations LEED Cx
Benefit Narrative: BAS review idnetified several issues with AHU dampers, heating and cooling valve positions. These cause operational problems such as deadheading a fan and can increase energy consumption by simultaneously heating and cooling at the air handler. implementing a sequence that prevents simulraneous heating and cooling, as well as headheading fans, will increase system performance and efficiency. Action;revise congrol logic of all AHUs to use a control amper and valve control sequence.

Currently both hot and chilled water secondary loop pump speeds are controlled to maintain a constant differential pressure setpoint. Implementing a DP setpoint reset strategy will reduce the setpoint duirng part load conditions, thus allowing the pumps to decrease their speed, save electrical energy and reduce wear/tear on pumps while still meeting the load demand of the system. Action: implement a secondary loop pump DP reset control strategy in the controls programming

column2: MMC R8 EP Lab Renovations Cx
Benefit Narrative: There is currently a secondary hot water temperature setpoint reset programmed to lower the hot water temperature when the outside air temperature increases. While this method works, it is not as effective at increasing efficiency as resetting the hot water temperature setpoint based on actual building demand as seen in the return water temperature. The current supply temperature setpoints also only modulate between 155°F and 180°F. This measure is to implement a hot water temperature setpoint reset based on demand and to widen the temperature setpoint range. Action: Implement hot water temperature setpoint reset programming that calculates a setpoint to meet the needs of the heating demand of the building and the snow melt system.

The condensing boilers, installed in the building, are designed to achieve their peak efficiency rating of 98% when they operate with condensing flue gas. For flue gas to condense, return water temperatures must be below 135°F and the boiler does not reach its peak efficiency until the return water temperature is about 80°F or lower. If the boiler operates outside of the condensing range, its operating efficiency is around 88%.Action: Implement a primary pump speed sequence that modulates the return hot water temperature such that the boiler operates in condensing mode.

column2: MMC AHU-72 and 73 Replacement Project Cx
Benefit Narrative: It was observed during the BAS review that both DHW pumps were on at the same time although one pump was commanded off. These pumps are designed to operate in a lead/lag fashion, where one operates while the other serves as a backup in the case of a lead pump failure. Action: Modify programming to operate DHW recirculation pumps in a lead/lag fashion

column2: MMC NDF Lab Exhaust Fan Reconfiguration Study/Design
Benefit Narrative: The existing chilled water system cools water to a manually input setpoint.This setpoint is changed by Spruce Camp staff depending on outside air conditions to meet the cooling demand of the building. The chiller will operate more efficiently as the temperature setpoint is increased. This temperature setpoint can be automated to reset based on current building cooling demand to optimize energy efficiency and performance.Action: Add programming to automatically reset the chilled water temperature setpoint based on the cooling demand of the building in order to maximize the chiller efficiency.

column2: MMC Green Roof AHU-22, Chiller, Split HVAC Relocation
Benefit Narrative: The condenser water temperature setpoint can be reset based on outside air temperature to maximize the chiller operating efficiency. Lowering the condenser water supply temperature automatically, when outside air conditions allow, will help increase chiller efficiency for all outside air temperature ranges except for the hottest design days. Action: Implement a condenser water supply temperature setpoint reset sequence to optimize the chiller efficiency.

The BAS review showed that VAV-2-15 has an unoccupied space heating temperature setpoint that conflicts with the occupied setpoint; namely, the occupied setpoint is below the unoccupied setpoint.Typically, unoccupied heating setpoints are a minimum of 5°F below the occupied heating setpoint, and 5°F above for cooling. The further these setpoints can be away from the occupied setpoints, the more opportunity there is to save energy during unoccupied hours. Additionally, if it is determined that all zones can be setback when the building is unoccupied, there is significant savings potential in shutting down air handlers during unoccupied hours.Action: Review all unoccupied setpoints and schedules to optimize the amount of time the building can be setback and the temperatures to which the building can be setback.

column2: National Bank of Middlebury HVAC Cx
Benefit Narrative: MAU-1 and MAU-2 are designed to provide makeup air to the kitchen exhaust hoods. During review of the BAS, it was found that MAU-2 was not in operation when its associated kitchen exhaust hoods were in operation, and that MAU-1 was only operating at ~900CFM with a 15% fan speed when enabled. Correcting the operation of these MAUs to operate when required and at the originally balanced setpoints will help the building pressurization and reduce load on AHU-2 and AHU-3. Another energy benefit of this measure is that MAU?s serving kitchens can typically be operated at more relaxed discharge air temperatures than an AHU serving other building spaces. This would decrease the overall heating and cooling loads on the building. Action: Determine issues with existing MAUs and rebalance to correct CFM to serve kitchen exhaust hoods. Optimize MAU discharge air temperature setpoints to reduce overall heating and cooling loads.

column2: Northeastern Vermont Regional Hospital (NVRH) Chilled Water RCx
Benefit Narrative: Spruce Camp staff noted that not all of the existing variable frequency drives have been integrated into the BAS graphics as there were specified to be. This leaves several drive displays on the BAS without valuable energy and operating data that could be trended and used for future analyses and trouble shooting. Action: Work with controls contractor to complete integration of all VFDs into the BAS graphics and verify values are correct.

The current design of the hot water piping system requires return heating hot water to flow through the heat exchanger for the snowmelt system, even when the snowmelt system is not active. This heat exchanger adds pressure drop to the system, which requires more pumping energy. There are also standby losses through this heat exchanger from heating the snowmelt loop water while it is not in use. If the heat exchanger could be bypassed when it is not in use, less pumping energy would be required and the heat loss due to heat transfer through the heat exchanger while it is idle would be eliminated. Action: Design and install piping and control valve to divert HW flow around snowmelt heat exchanger when not in use. Program valve in BAS to open to the heat exchanger when snowmelt is activated.

column2: VA Medical Center Leeds MA Building 5 Kitchen Reno Cx
Benefit Narrative: Spruce Camp described issues with the snowmelt system during large snowstorms. At these times, a majority of the heating capacity is diverted to the snowmelt system and the hot water system cannot meet the building loads. This could be due to the system not being balanced and the flow diverting completely through the snowmelt heat exchanger during high demand. There also may be an opportunity to improve efficiency of the snowmelt system through zone pump control. All pumps currently have VFDs, but they are set for constant speeds when operating. Action: An in-depth study should be conducted for the snowmelt system controls and interaction with the building heating loop to identify opportunities to improve energy efficiency and performance of the system.

The existing BAS schedule for the occupied mode is from 6:00 a.m. to 9:00 p.m. seven days a week. Based on discussions with building occupants and facility staff, these operating hours are longer than needed. Tightening the building operating schedule would result in significant fan energy savings, some steam and chilled water savings, and reduced wear on mechanical equipment. Savings for this measure are estimated by assuming the building starts up an hour later and shuts down an hour earlier on weekdays (schedule of 7:00 a.m. ? 8:00 p.m.) and modifies the weekend schedule to be 10:00 a.m. ? 5:00 p.m. This schedule was developed with input from the UVM staff.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Shut Down ERV-1 During Unoccupied Hours
Benefit Narrative: .Currently the main energy recovery unit serving the building (ERV-1) does not shut down completely during unoccupied periods, but instead runs at its minimum speed. This operation was initially put in place to address issues with stale smells in the building, but according to building staff and occupants this issue has since been resolved with the addition of the 2,500 CFM exhaust fan. This measure is to put ERV-1 on its own schedule and to shut the unit down completely during unoccupied hours. Heating and cooling systems are typically started up 1-2 hours before occupants are expected in the building, in order to ensure that spaces are at comfortable temperature by the time people arrive. However, since ventilation air is not required during unoccupied hours, the unit does not need to run during morning warm-up or cool-down. Energy savings are therefore generated by reducing the daily operating hours of ERV-1.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Reduce Exhaust Fan Speed During Unoccupied Hours
Benefit Narrative: The relatively new exhaust fan serving the toilet and janitor areas currently runs continuously 24/7 at 100% speed. This measure reduces the fan speed during unoccupied hours while bathroom and janitor area use is expected to be minimal or nonexistent.  By keeping the fan operating at a reduced speed while the ERV is off during unoccupied hours, the toilet, soil storage, and janitor areas will maintain a negative pressure to the building, thus avoiding odors from migrating to the rest of the building.

Currently the CO2 level setpoint is set to 900 ppm, above which VAV boxes will modulate open to introduce more ventilation air and cause ERV-1 fan speeds to increase. This setpoint could be raised to 1,100 ppm, which would be consistent with the UVM standard for other campus buildings and result in a lower volume of outside air introduced into the building. Reducing the amount of outside air that requires conditioning would result in heating, cooling, and fan energy savings.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Run Fan Coil Units in Cycle Mode
Benefit Narrative: The fan coil units can run in either ?Cycle? or ?FAN ON? mode, and it is estimated that many of the units are currently manually operating in ?FAN ON? mode, in which the fan operates continuously when the spaces are occupied. In ?Cycle? mode, the fan only cycles ?on? in order to provide heating or cooling to meet the space temperature setpoint.  Since ventilation air is provided through a separate system, running the fans continuously in not necessary, and ensuring that all FCUs are operating in ?Cycle? mode could lead to significant fan energy savings.

The heat recovery ventilator serving the eco-machine room currently runs continuously 24/7. This measure shuts this unit off during unoccupied hours. Since there is hot water baseboard radiation serving this area, the HRV is not required and the baseboard can maintain the unoccupied heating temperature setpoint

During our building walk-through, we discovered that pump CP-8 was running, which serves the 4-pipe fan coil unit chilled water loop, even though there was no cooling requirement at the time in the spaces served. Facilities staff then informed us that this pump runs continuously 24/7. This measure adjusts the BAS programming so that the pump only runs when a fan coil unit served is calling for cooling.

column2: UVM RSENR Ecosystem Science Lab RCx
Benefit Narrative: The current exhaust fans operate on a constant static pressure and modulate the outside air damper within the plenum. By installing a VFD on the existing fans, the outside air bypass can be removed, and the fans will only exhaust fume hood air. The fans can operate on duct static pressure differential and the airflow volume will be reduced dramatically.Technically a capital improvement rather than a retrocommissioning measure, this modification carries the most energy savings potential of the measures identified and has a very quick payback.

column2: UVM RSENR Ecosystem Science Lab RCx
Benefit Narrative:
The existing 3-way valves at the VAV boxes prohibit the hot water pump from modulating to meet actual conditioning loads. The constant recirculation of the hot water also increases the return water to the boiler and prohibits the boiler from operating in condensing mode. Replacing these pneumatic valves with 2-way electronic valves will enable the hot water pumps to modulate on its own internal controls to meet the building loads and return the water at more appropriate temperatures. A lower hot water return temperature would enable the condensing boiler to operate at, or close to, its rated efficiency of 96.7% as compared to its current operating efficiency of 88.4%. Additionally, the air compressor will be able to operate at significantly lower loads and potentially a lower pressure setpoint if these valves are removed.
Not a retro-commissioning measure, as it involves a capital investment, we highly recommend implementing this measure. Savings are conservative

column2: UVM RSENR Ecosystem Science Lab RCx
Benefit Narrative: If the laboratory end-use processes will allow it, we recommend implementing a min/max range for each space thermostat so that the spaces do not have large extremes and the building reduces its simultaneous heating and cooling. During our site visit, one room was set to 60°F, causing the ERV DAT to be 55°F (instead of 65°F) while all other VAV boxes were in heating mode. At the same time one space thermostat, the wet room, was set to 80°F.

column2: UVM RSENR Ecosystem Science Lab RCx
Benefit Narrative: The current nighttime unoccupied setpoint is 63°F. Unless the ecological processes cannot accept lower temperatures, we recommend lowering this temperature to 60°F for all appropriate spaces (assumed to be 80% of the building for the savings calculations).

column2: UVM RSENR Ecosystem Science Lab RCx
Benefit Narrative: One (of a total of two) heat pump remains operating but we could not trace where this unit is serving. There is a large possibility the spaces were reconfigured, and this unit is no longer necessary. UVM can consider turning the unit off and determining if a space is negatively affected.

column2: UVM RSENR Ecosystem Science Lab RCx
Benefit Narrative: This unoccupied demand seems high and may be indicative of malfunctioning HVAC occupancy sensors that do not enable the VAV boxes to go to the lower airflow. This would increase the ERV, chiller, boiler demands unnecessarily. We recommend trending the VAV box setpoints to determine if they modulate to their minimum setpoint in unoccupied mode during the day and at night. We also recommend testing the sensors by walking into an unoccupied room that is operating at its minimum and determining if the VAV damper modulates open to the occupied setpoint

Benefit: Benefit Name: Differential Pressure Reset on HWP-3 and HWP-4
Benefit Narrative: HWP-3 and HWP-4 are the secondary pumps, which circulate hot water throughout most of the building. They have VFDs and their speed is controlled to maintain a fixed 7 PSI differential pressure (dP) setpoint. This measure would reset the dP setpoint based on zone conditions. On a cold day, the pumps would maintain 7 PSI. On moderate days, the pressure setpoint would be lowered. This measure will reduce pumping energy use.

column2: Montpelier Roxbury Public Schools - Montpelier Union Elementary School RCx
Benefit: Benefit Name: Reprogram Unit Ventilator Fan and Valve Control
Benefit Narrative: At night, the Unit Ventilators throughout the building often go into heating mode, which opens the hot water valves 100% but does not turn on the fan (eventually the fan turns on). This measure involves reprogramming the units so that when the zone calls for heat in unoccupied mode, the fan turns on and the valve opens simultaneously. This should improve heat exchange, reduce pumping energy, and improve the delta T of the hot water loop (thereby increasing the system efficiency).

column2: Montpelier Roxbury Public Schools - Montpelier Union Elementary School RCx
Benefit: Benefit Name: AHU-1 and AHU-2 Demand Controlled Ventilation
Benefit Narrative: AHU-1 and AHU-2 have a fixed minimum outside air damper position. This means that whenever the units are on, they bring in outside air even if the spaces are empty. This measure would involve using the CO2 sensor as an indication of occupancy, and adjusting the minimum damper position based on the CO2. This measure can save energy be reducing the outside air heating load when the space is not occupied. This measure also ensures adequate ventilation by increasing the outside percentage when the space is heavily occupied and CO2 levels rise. It appears that the logic already exists for this measure so this will be a fairly straightforward measure.

column2: Montpelier Roxbury Public Schools - Montpelier Union Elementary School RCx
Benefit: Benefit Name: AHU-1 and AHU-2 Minimum Fan Speed Adjustment
Benefit Narrative:
The AHUs have a minimum fan speed of 50%. When the units are heating, this is likely necessary in order to properly mix the air in the space and prevent warm supply air from stratifying and staying near the ceiling. However, when the AHU is not heating or when a moderate (70°F-80°F) supply air temperature is required, the supply air is more likely to mix with the room air and the minimum fan speed could likely be reduced. Note that this measure should be implemented with ECM-3 so that the demand controlled sequence will ensure that the spaces have adequate outside air during periods of high occupancy.

column2: Montpelier Roxbury Public Schools - Montpelier Union Elementary School RCx
Benefit Narrative: The ALC control system is capable of incorporating holiday schedules. While on site, Josh Dalmer showed the staff the scheduling features of ALC. We recommend a quick training for all staff. We also recommend assigning holiday scheduling to a staff member and setting reminders throughout the year. Holidays can be programmed in advance, so once the school calendar is set, the major holidays could be programmed.

column2: Montpelier Roxbury Public Schools - Montpelier Union Elementary School RCx
Benefit Narrative: Optimal Start/Stop is a control feature that uses historical trend data from outdoor and indoor temperatures to determine the appropriate equipment start time prior to a building?s occupied mode. The idea is that on a cold morning, it will start the equipment earlier so that the space is up to temperature by the scheduled occupancy time. We recommend a review of the optimal start parameters and scheduled start times for all the AHUs and terminal equipment. We observed equipment or occupied setpoints being enabled at 2AM in order to prepare for a 6AM occupancy period. This may be needed at times, but based on trends it appears that most equipment is capable of quickly bringing spaces up to occupied temperature setpoints.

column2: Montpelier Roxbury Public Schools - Montpelier Union Elementary School RCx
Benefit: Benefit Name: Storage Room 100 (FTR-1) Temperature Setpoint and Control
Benefit Narrative: The storage room is unoccupied most of the time and unless sensitive supplies are stored there, the space temperature setpoint (both occupied and unoccupied) could be lowered. In addition, the fan coil heater frequently turns on for 3 minutes (about 100 fan starts per day). We recommend adjusting parameters to prevent short cycling. Staff could also consider if any other zones in the building are similar and could utilize lower heating setpoints.

column2: Montpelier Roxbury Public Schools - Montpelier Union Elementary School RCx
Benefit Narrative:
Currently, the radiant heat is disabled when the outside air temperature exceeds 65°F. It could likely be locked out at a lower temperature, perhaps 50°F or 55°F. In addition, if people are not spending much time in the vestibule, it could likely be heated to a lower temperature (current setpoint is 70°F during the day). Note: Vermont code recommends a maximum of 55°F setpoint for vestibules. Staff noted issues with this space overheating on moderate days. In May, we observed the room exceeding 85F with the radiant heat operating, so we believe there are significant energy savings and comfort improvement opportunities.

A related issue is the exhaust fan control. Staff mentioned that the vestibule overheated on moderate days in the Fall and the exhaust fan did not turn on to cool the space. The logic will not let the exhaust fan run if the radiant floor pump is on. The control logic should be reviewed but reducing the outside air cutout and heating setpoint for the radiant system would likely help prevent overheating the space on moderate days.

column2: Montpelier Roxbury Public Schools - Montpelier Union Elementary School RCx
Benefit: Benefit Name: AHU-1 and AHU-2 Supply Air Temperatrure Control
Benefit Narrative: AHU-1 and AHU-2 use the room temperature PID loop to directly control the heating valve. One challenge with this type of control is that it does not easily allow us to maintain the temperature within a specific range. Trends indicate that supply air temperature has often exceeded 150°F. At that temperature, the air is likely to stratify, with warm air staying near the ceiling, and will not effectively mix the air in the room. We recommend reprogramming the AHUs to use a cascading loop; the room temperature control would be used to reset a supply air temperature setpoint. The hot water valve would modulate to maintain the supply air temperature setpoint, and the control logic could set maximum and minimum limits on the supply air temperature.

column2: Montpelier Roxbury Public Schools - Montpelier Union Elementary School RCx
Benefit: Benefit Name: Ahu-1 and AHU-2 Nighttime VFD Speed and HHW Control Valve
Benefit Narrative: At night, when these units turn on, they go to 100% speed and 100% valve position immediately. This allows them to quickly heat the space, but they only run about 5 minutes and often turn on about 10 times each night. With such a short run time, the air likely is not effectively mixed in the room. This measure would test out different parameters to gradually ramp the units and run them a bit longer when needed. Running the fans at a lower speed saves energy, but this measure mostly targets reduced wear and tear on motors/actuators.

column2: Montpelier Roxbury Public Schools - Montpelier Union Elementary School RCx
Benefit Narrative: The building sets back the heating temperature setpoint throughout the building. There may be opportunity to set the temperature back more in some spaces. Currently, most spaces heat to 65°F at night. That is probably appropriate for rooms that are slow to heat back up in the morning. However, if there are rooms that easily meet their temperature setpoint first thing in the morning, it may be possible to set back the temperature a bit more. We recommend gradually testing setback temperatures and monitoring the spaces.

column2: Montpelier Roxbury Public Schools - Montpelier Union Elementary School RCx
Benefit Narrative: Some of the cabinet unit heaters appear to be short cycling. The fan in CUH-2 appears to only run for 1 minute when it turns on. When the HHW valve opens, it goes to 100% and also only stays open briefly. We recommend checking the PID parameters and considering minimum run times or hysteresis to help reduce short cycling.

column2: Montpelier Roxbury Public Schools - Montpelier Union Elementary School RCx
Benefit Narrative: HWP-1, which circulates water through the heat exchanger on the building side, enables when outside air temperature is less than 65°F. The building appears to have little to no heating needs when outside air temperature is ~60°F. This measure would involve building staff lowering the cutout temperature by a few degrees, which would save pumping and possibly heating energy.

column2: Montpelier Roxbury Public Schools - Montpelier Union Elementary School RCx
Benefit: Benefit Name: Access Additional Hot Water Piping Configurationi
Benefit Narrative: The piping in the addition has equipment from various ages and serves both heating and domestic hot water loads. There appear to be multiple bypasses in the system between the district heat and the end uses in the addition. This measure would trace out the piping and confirm which bypasses are needed. It would also investigate the domestic water plumbing (staff noted that there have been issues with domestic hot water in this area). If bypasses can be eliminated, there would be a pumping energy reduction.

column2: Montpelier Roxbury Public Schools - Montpelier Union Elementary School RCx
Benefit Narrative: The Cafeteria AHU has face and bypass control and does not have any control valve for the hot water coil. Hot water is always flowing through the coil. When the AHU is heating, air is diverted through the coil. When the AHU does not need to heat, air is diverted around the coil. This sequence is needed to prevent freezing the coil because it is not a glycol system. However, there is a lot of wasted energy because, for example, when the AHU is off, hot water continues to heat the AHU. Trends indicate that this Winter, the unit was heated up to about 90°F at night when the unit was not operating. In addition, even though the unit has bypass dampers, there is likely some heat leaking into the airstream on moderate days, which could cause spaces to overheat.

column2: Montpelier Roxbury Public Schools - Montpelier Union Elementary School RCx
Benefit Narrative: The building has several (about 5-8 exhaust fans), but only EF-4, the kitchen exhaust fan, the auditorium exhaust fan, and the gym exhaust fan are integrated with the BAS. This measure would investigate the other exhaust fans and what they serve. If they can be turned off at night, it may be worthwhile to integrate them with the BAS so they can be scheduled on/off each day.

column2: Montpelier Roxbury Public Schools - Montpelier Union Elementary School RCx
Benefit: Benefit Name: Gym and Small Gym Motion Sensors
Benefit Narrative: The gym and small gym have motion sensor for lights. This measure would investigate integrating the motion sensors into the BAS. Then the sequence would be modified so that if the space was empty during normal occupied, the AHU could be put into a standby mode. Normally, standby mode adjusts the setpoint by a couple degrees so that the space can quickly get back up to temperature when someone enters the room.

column2: Montpelier Roxbury Public Schools - Montpelier Union Elementary School RCx
Benefit Narrative: MRPS staff noted that the district heat bills are quite high for this building compared to other MRPS buildings that heat with fuel oil. It is possible that there could be billing errors. This measure would investigate the district heat bills. We would check the district heat metering to ensure that MRPS is being billed for the appropriate quantity of heat. We would also review the billing methodology to ensure that the bill dollar amounts are being calculated correctly.

column2: Montpelier Roxbury Public Schools - Montpelier Union Elementary School RCx
Benefit: Benefit Name: District Heat Minimum Valve Postion and Minimum VFD Speed
Benefit Narrative:
We understand from TCV that when the controls were set up, the district heat operators wanted to maintain a particular return water temperature and a minimum flow rate. On the district side of the heat exchangers, there is a control valve that normally has a minimum position of 25%. However, the sequence often does not maintain flow on the district side. The heat exchanger often delivers more heat than the building needs, and eventually the valve closes and there is no flow on the district side of the loop. We recommend discussing the specific requirements with the district heat operators. It may be beneficial to reduce the minimum valve position, which would allow the district flow to modulate more rather than shutting off and turning on repeatedly. 

Additionally, HWP-1, which circulates water through the heat exchanger on the building side, has a minimum speed of 17%. Depending on the motor ratings, it may be possible to reduce the minimum speed of the pump. Again, this would help the pump modulate for more hours rather than shutting on/off repeatedly.

column2: Montpelier Roxbury Public Schools - Montpelier Union Elementary School RCx
Benefit: Benefit Name: Replace HWP-1 and HWP-1 With Smartpumps
Benefit Narrative: HWP-1, HWP-2, HWP-3, and HWP-4 all serve a similar function but while HWP-3 and HWP-4 have VFDs, HWP-1 and HWP-2 do not. The pumps are constant volume. This measure would modulate the pump speed.  Traditionally, this measure would involve installing VFDs and associated controls. However, we believe that a more cost-effective approach would be to replace the pumps with new ?smart? pumps, which have built-in speed control and do not require a separate VFD or controls sensors. We believe the capital cost of this measure would be slightly lower than a VFD installation and another benefit is that the school would get brand new pumps (whereas VFDs would continue using the existing pumps). We strongly urge commissioning of these pumps as smartpumps are often installed without proper programming of their modulation.

column2: Montpelier Roxbury Public Schools - Montpelier Union Elementary School RCx
Benefit Narrative: There are opportunities for insulation and air sealing in several locations. The bulkhead doors leading to the basement are leaky. Pipe insulation appeared to be good, but there were a few sections missing insulation. Lastly, the foundation walls appeared to be uninsulated and would benefit from insulation and air sealing. This is not a retrocommissioning measure, but we suggest discussing it with Efficiency Vermont as there may be incentives available.

column2: RRMC AHU-3 System Replacement and Improvements
Benefit Narrative: The project was completed in two stages. During the first stage, the AHU operated with a minimum outdoor air flow. During the second stage, and HRV was installed to provide OA to the AHU, but the OA damper was not closed and left at the position set at stage 1. This measure ensured that the damper is closed.

column2: VT Transco New Haven Operations Facility Cx
Benefit Narrative: The project design called for an airflow measuring station for measuring outside airflow for the 2 AHUs serving this building. During FPT, we found that these had never been installed because there wasn't enough straight run to install. However, this was never brought up to the team, so if we hadn't found this out during FPT, the AFMSs may never have been installed. This is still an ongoing issue (there really isn't enough straight run for these to be installed), but at least the team is now aware and is working on solutions.

Miscellaneous air sealing opportunities of an already decent performing warehouse building. Measures included primary overhead and person door weather-stripping as well as select air sealing at the wall base.

column2: Jay Peak Resort Waste Heat Recovery Design and Cx
Benefit Narrative: Water-to-water heat pumps were installed to recovery heat from the hotel heat pump loop to use to provide heat for water park HRUs and water features. Benefits include reduced propane consumption for boilers and reduced cooling tower electricity consumption. There is an overall increase in electric use due to the WWHP operation.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Bronch Exhaust not compliant in design documents
Benefit Narrative: During our design review, we brought up that the Bronch Exhaust didn't align with ASHRAE 170 requirements - ASHRAE 170-2017 6.3.2.2 defines that bronch exhaust shall discharge to the atmosphere in a vertical direction at least 10FT above adjoining roof level with no rain cap. The original design had the horizontal discharge on side of building through louver. Additionally ASHRAE 170-2017 6.3.2.1.a defines that all ductwork within the building is under negative pressure. The original exhaust fan location resulted in positive pressure in building above a ceiling in the corridor. Both of these items had to be addressed in the design, but luckily this could occur early on in the project and didn't impact the project schedule.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Optimize RTU Supply Air Temperature Control
Benefit Narrative:
According to the BAS graphics and the controls submittal, the supply air temperature setpoint for RTU-1 and RTU-2 is reset between 55°F and 65°F. The written sequence of operation from the controls submittal notes that the setpoint will be ?reset automatically based on VAV zone demand?, but provides no further detail on how this is programmed. Through the review of BAS trend data, it appears that this supply air temperature control sequence could be optimized to reduce energy consumption.

In general, the setpoint is slightly below 60°F when outdoor air temperatures are low and actually increases slightly at higher outdoor air temperatures. This may be resulting in significant reheat energy at the VAV boxes during the winter months, which could be reduced with a higher supply air temperature from the RTUs.

We recommend implementing a supply air temperature reset control sequence based on ASHRAE Guideline 368, which is considered current best practice. This involves instituting trim-and-respond control logic for the supply air temperature setpoint, whereby heating and/or cooling requests are generated by each zone based on the zone temperature deviation from setpoint, and the supply air temperature setpoint is either raised or lowered by a set amount based on the number of requests.

Currently RTU-1 and RTU-2 have minimum outside air damper positions of 10% and 30% respectively. Figure 11 and Figure 12 plot the outside air damper position vs. outdoor air temperature for RTU-1 and RTU-2. The OA damper for RTU-1 is commanded open to relatively high levels at fairly cold outdoor air temperatures to meet the supply air temperature setpoint, and then goes to minimum position when the economizer is disabled.

For RTU-2, it appears that at high outdoor air temperatures, the outside air damper may have been manually overridden to very low values.

It is unclear how the existing minimum outdoor air damper positions were determined. We suspect the 30% minimum for RTU-2 may be higher than necessary. We recommend calculating actual ventilation requirements for the building using ASHRAE Standard 62.1 ? Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor Air Quality. This procedure determines the necessary amount of ventilation air to introduce at the RTUs based on the space types served, floor area, and number of occupants. Once the proper airflow rate is calculated, a TAB contractor can determine the actual minimum damper position that will correspond to the necessary airflow.

We also recommend performing point-to-point testing to confirm the dampers are modulating correctly and match the positions shown in the BAS.

Additionally, ventilation only needs to be provided when there are occupants present in the building - outside air is not required during morning warmup or cooldown prior to occupancy10. We recommend implementing a morning warmup cycle for the RTUs, whereby units will operate with outdoor air dampers closed upon startup for an hour or so prior to occupants entering the building, after which outside air dampers will modulate open to minimum position. The outside air damper can also be closed when the unit cycles on during unoccupied periods to maintain the unoccupied temperature setpoint.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Chilled Water Loop Differential Pressure Reset
Benefit Narrative: The chilled water pump currently operates to maintain a fixed differential pressure setpoint in the chilled water loop. While this setpoint may be appropriate for periods when maximum cooling is required, the setpoint can be reset lower during part load conditions. This can be done through implementing ?trim and respond? control logic (similar to the supply air temperature reset measure), in which the differential pressure setpoint is reset between a minimum and maximum based on the position of control valves in the loop, while maintaining any required minimum flow through the chillers.

Benefit: Benefit Name: Optimize Control of Hot Water Loop Pumps
Benefit Narrative: Based on trend data analysis, the hot water loop pump appears to operate continuously 24/7 throughout the year (Figure 13). During unoccupied periods over the summer and shoulder seasons, it is unlikely that heat would be required at all times in the building and the pump could be turned off. We recommend enabling the pump only when there is a call for heating from the building during unoccupied hours and during occupied hours when outside air temperatures are above 55°F. For occupied hours below 55°F, the pump would operate continuously.

Occasionally, BAS overrides are put in place by facilities managers in order to temporarily solve a problem or address an issue and are left in place after the issue has been resolved. We only noticed one override in place ? the minimum flow for VAV-3 was overridden to 500 cfm. If there is no longer any need for this override, we suggest releasing it to normal operation, as high minimum flow values can potentially contribute to increased fan and chiller energy and reheat energy consumption.

column2: MVZ Holdings HVAC and Refrigeration System Assessment
Benefit Narrative:
Refrigeration Rack System
Currently, the three walk-in coolers in the building are each served by a separate compressor, which rejects heat into the space where it is located. Installing a centralized refrigeration rack system would allow for the installation of a larger and therefore more efficient compressor to serve all three walk-ins. Significant savings can be realized by replacing the existing reciprocating compressors with new efficient scroll compressors. Savings would also result from rejecting heat to the outdoors during colder weather rather than the indoors. In addition, a central rack system would allow for a more controlled way to reject heat. For example, heat can be rejected to the outdoors if no heating is required in the building, or can be recovered, potentially as the first stage of heat in a space or to preheat domestic hot water.

Efficient Evaporator Fans and Evaporator Fan Control
Evaporator fans serving walk-in coolers and freezers typically run continuously, 24/7. These fans not only consume electricity while in operation, they also create heat that must be removed by the refrigeration system. There are two common ways that energy use by evaporator fans can be reduced. The first is to replace traditional shaded-pole motors with higher efficiency brushless DC motors, also known as electronically commutated (ECM) motors. The second method is to control the fan speed. Although evaporator fans run continuously, the refrigeration circuit is only operating part of the time. The rest of the time, the evaporator fans are just circulating air. Evaporator fan speed controllers allow fans to operate at low speed when the refrigeration circuit is off, saving additional energy.
We recommend installing efficient evaporator fans with ECM motors wherever feasible. We also recommend installing evaporator fan speed controllers such that the fans can operate at a lower speed during periods when the refrigeration circuit is not operational.

column2: MVZ Holdings HVAC and Refrigeration System Assessment
Benefit Narrative: The building walkthrough revealed numerous opportunities to improve building maintenance. Consider implementing a preventative maintenance (PM) plan. A PM plan gives the building owner a timeline of how often the equipment in the building should be re-visited for regular maintenance to optimize its functionality. The PM plan should include items like changing filters, tightening fan belts, checking equipment functionality, cleaning refrigeration coils, tuning boilers, etc. Having a PM plan in place, whether internal or contracted out, will improve the lifespan of the building equipment, improve their functionality and efficacy, and improve occupant comfort.

column2: MVZ Holdings HVAC and Refrigeration System Assessment
Benefit Narrative: One of the evident issues found during the building walkthrough was that filters were either very dirty, did not fit properly, or were not installed at all. We recommend replacing filters on a regular basis (every 3-6 months) to improve air quality and decrease stress on AHU fans. We also recommend ensuring the filters fit properly so that air cannot bypass the filter medium.

column2: MVZ Holdings HVAC and Refrigeration System Assessment
Benefit Narrative: The primary kitchen exhaust hood in the Kingdom Table kitchen has variable frequency drives (VFDs) on the fan motors, meaning the fan speed can modulate. However, the fan speed is controlled by a dial on the wall, which kitchen staff set at a certain speed each shift and don?t change. We recommend removing these speed control dials and instead adding an interlocked temperature and/or infrared sensor that directs the kitchen exhaust to modulate with the load based on the heat coming off the cooking surface and/or level of steam and smoke. This will result in decreased total run times at the maximum speed, saving energy over the life of the exhaust fan.

column2: MVZ Holdings HVAC and Refrigeration System Assessment
Benefit Narrative: Ventilation to all the occupied spaces is critical for a healthy building and at the moment there is insufficient mechanical ventilation being brought into the building. We recommend installing an energy recovery unit for each tenant suite in the building as the lowest cost option for providing ventilation air. The spaces will use the other HVAC equipment for heating and cooling; the ERV will just provide the correct amount of outside air per space at a neutral temperature. For most spaces, this will require adding ductwork that leads outside, but each space?s ERV can tie into the existing AHUs? ductwork for distribution within the space. Installing a separate ERV for each tenant space means the ventilation costs are passed onto the tenant rather than to MVZ Holdings.

We recommended adjusting the summer outdoor design conditions from 85F dry bulb/69F wet bulb to 90F dry bulb/74F wet bulb. Humidity levels had been brought up by the owner as a concern, and at the originally proposed design conditions, there was likelihood that there would be humidity issues. Additionally, with climate change greatly affecting Burlington summer temperatures, a 90F dry bulb day is becoming more and more common, so using these parameters means the space will be more resilient over the long term.

column2: Pen Bay Medical Center ED Renovation and Expansion Cx
Benefit: Benefit Name: AII vs. Negative Pressure Rooms in Design
Benefit Narrative: Original design had negative pressure rooms, but wouldn't satisfy Airborne Infection Isolation (AII) room requirements. When CxA brought this issue up during our first design review, the hospital indicated these should be AII and changes needed to be made. If CxA hadn't brought this up, the hospital would not have the room types they needed.

column2: Pen Bay Medical Center ED Renovation and Expansion Cx
Benefit: Benefit Name: Exhaust fans proximity to AHUs OA intakes
Benefit Narrative: From the design review - Several Exhaust Fans were too close to the AHUs OA Intakes. ASHRAE 170 spells out requirements, but AII and ER Waiting Room exhaust is 25' horizontally, and also must be discharged 10' above roof. 30' from the discharge of AII EFs (Hypotenuse) and other fans are too close. The design was updated to align with ASHRAE 170 and catching this during design review prevented a bigger issue down the road.

column2: State of Maine Greenlaw Building Renovation Cx
Benefit Narrative: Hot water and energy recovery coils were found to be piped differently than the design details.  In addition, the design details didn't specify a required reverse return piping arrangement on multiple coil banks.  We identified the piping issue and provided input to the design team and the contractor on piping arrangement to resolve the issues.  Identifying during construction prevented the need for rework at the end of the construction phase and potential postponing of occupancy to make necessary modifications.

column2: DHMC Specialty Pharmacy and Supply Chain Cx
Benefit Narrative: The hot water distribution pumps for the boiler 1 and 2 heating loop were specified to enable any time the outside air temperatures were below 60F. This would have resulted in the pumps running many more hours of the year than needed, which increases both energy use and strain on the pumps. We recommended including the additional requirement of a call for heat from an AHU control valve or DDC monitoring device in order for the hot water pumps to enable. This change was relayed to the controls contractor and will result in significantly decreased run-hours over the lives of these pumps.

column2: Paul J. Schupf Art Center Cx
Benefit Narrative: From a PFC site visit, it was noted that the chiller which is located on the roof was mounted without vibration isolation (springs) which was required per the specifications. This could have resulted in potential vibration/sound issues. The contractor will be installing the appropriate equipment.

column2: Paul J. Schupf Art Center Cx
Benefit Narrative: From PFC - the installed hot water pumps were 1HP instead of 1.5HP per the design/submittal. This could have created potential capacity/flow issues once the building was occupied. The contractor has replaced these pumps with the correct ones.

column2: Paul J. Schupf Art Center Cx
Benefit Narrative: The pumps had been submitted without bacnet cards even though they require integration. We recommended re-submitting w/bacnet

column2: Groundworks Collaborative 60 South Main Street Envelope Cx
Benefit: Benefit Name: US Passive House (PHIUS) Equivalent Air Barrier
Benefit Narrative: Commissioning efforts included installation verification visits, qualitative air barrier testing using theatrical fog, and compliance blower door testing and infrared imaging. The efforts of the BECx process helped yield blower door test results that met, and surpassed, equivalent air barrier thresholds set for 2021 US Passive House (PHIUS) performance standards (0.06 CFM50/SF, 0.08 CFM75/SF). Significant discoveries as a part of the commissioning process included window opening air leakage between air barrier and weather barrier sill flashings, ceiling air barrier cuts, tears, and disconnects at the perimeter, and potential warranty implications relating to the use of air barrier accessory materials.

column2: SVMC Chilled Water System Capacity Assessment
Benefit: Benefit Name: CHW Piping for greater chiller delta T
Benefit Narrative: We conducted an in depth review of the CHW plant and loads at SVMC. One outcome was that SVMC has acted on our recommendation to repipe a portion of the return which will increase the return water temps to the CHW plant. This wasn't a true RCx project but this seems the most appropriate phase. This will increase overall plant capacity while likely reducing the kW/Ton of the plant. We have pre and post implementation data from the plant and determining accurate savings should be straight forward.

column2: National Bank of Middlebury Seymour Street Branch Consulting
Benefit Narrative: Replacing an R-22 split AC system with an ASHP with integrated controls for the boiler. Below 37F (economic balance point), the boiler operates, above 37F the Heat Pump operates

column2: UVMMC 192 Tilley Drive 3T MRI Addition Cx
Benefit Narrative: This project is the addition of a new MRI, making a new exam room and equipment room. The rooms need to follow ASHRAE 170 guidelines since it's a medical space. However, we found during submittal review that the RTU they spec'd did not have outside air at all. This resulted in them spec'ing an economizer accessory to allow introduction of OA.

column2: UVMMC 192 Tilley Drive 3T MRI Addition Cx
Benefit Narrative: We recommended sealing lineset insulation for outdoor refrigerant piping b/c we've seen pitting & leaks in copper piping from water getting beneath the insulation. This can lead to refrigerant leaks. 3 benefits - lower maintenance costs (don't need to replace piping), lower operations cost (don't need to replace refrigerant), and prevention of GHG emissions (R410 and its awful GWP!!)

Exterior infrared imaging revealed relatively significant conductivity around the perimeter of the building at the exposed slab edge/frost wall, as evidenced by the images below.

This is not uncommon to see for foundation/slab systems that are uninsulated. However, given much of the slab in this building contains radiant tube heat, the impacts on heat loss through the slab edge and on radiant distribution efficiency are more substantial. One small portion of the building, on the west side of the bakery space, has been insulated at the perimeter with 2? of extruded polystyrene (XPS) insulation. The extent of this insulation below grade is unknown. The difference in conductive heat loss can easily be seen in the following photo set.

We recommend that the entire slab edge/frost wall perimeter be insulated to code minimums with XPS (extruded polystyrene), GPS (graphite polystyrene), or high-density EPS (expanded polystyrene) rigid board insulation. Code minimum is currently R-20 for heated slabs and slab perimeters. XPS is the highest performing product in terms of R-value per inch, but the least environmentally friendly in terms of embodied carbon. GPS and EPS insulations have much smaller embodied carbon numbers but offer less R-value per inch of insulation. That said GPS insulation is a good compromise between XPS and EPS in terms of R-value, cost, and friendliness to the environment.
Cx Associates recommends 4-5? of insulation extending from the top of the frost wall to the footing. Insulations with premanufactured drainage grooves may be considered depending on the backfill utilized. Alternatively, you may choose to insulate only the above grade portions of the frost wall, which will save on material and labor costs, but will have a reduced effect on long-term energy savings.
Given the exposure of insulation above grade, and the protrusion beyond the plane of the framed wall, implementation should also consider a UV resistant finish (ex. stucco, vinyl/metal coil stock, etc.) as well as a drip cap flashing integrated with the wall so that water flashes out over the front of the insulation and not behind it.

Most overhead doors are uninsulated, likely due to the original nature of the building as an unconditioned space. The minimal R-value of the doors is established largely by the air films on either side of the door rather than the door itself. Additionally, most doors exhibited significant damage-related concerns at the perimeter that have a direct impact on the doors? performance from an air leakage or operability standpoint.

Between the low R-value and associated air leakage, we estimate these overhead doors collectively have a significant impact on the building?s energy consumption, total measured air infiltration, and the general cooling the building experiences on windy days.

Given the low R-value, poor operability, and poor general condition of overhead doors, Cx Associates recommends all overhead doors be replaced. First consider any doors which may not be necessary to present or future operations. Are there any that can be removed and infilled with insulated wall instead? There was uncertainty when asking this question during our analysis phase but realize that the best overhead door is unlikely to outperform a well-insulated wall. For example, the overhead door in the aquaculture space does not appear to be utilized given the insulation stuffed into the door head and the amount of equipment interior and exterior of it. Could this be infilled with insulated wall? Energy modeling assumes all overhead doors are simply replaced, so infill of overhead doors will only yield additional savings.

Given most overhead doors do not appear to be frequently utilized (meaning approximately 10 or more open/close cycles each day), it means most doors will perform more like walls most of the time than they will doors. Therefore, we recommend standard speed, thermally insulated overhead doors. Standard speed doors, versus high speed, are more readily available with higher R-value and lower air infiltration ratings. Target an R-15+ overhead door, ideally without any lites within the sections. Given the mixed-use of the building and potential for future changes, we suggest a heavy-duty door. Overhead Door Company makes several commercial door models that fit these criteria. Should the overhead door of the aqua-culture space be replaced (rather than infilled), we recommend the high R-value model 850 door by Overhead Door Company or a rubber-based high-speed door, given the high humidity environment. The former is steel clad but contains an R-value of up to R-26, reducing condensation potential on its interior surface. The latter is an exterior-rated durable rubber material that will not rust as a result of high humidity, and its high-speed characteristics will reduce heat lost by air leakage during its open/close duration. The drawback is that it does not boast any significant R-value due to its construction. Practically speaking, this may not be a significant concern given the residual heat put into this space as a result of the heated process tanks which are served by a boiler.

Person swing doors typically exhibit a variety of conditions in which doors, hardware, and/or weather-stripping are damaged, rusted, or missing to varying degrees. While the total square footage of the swing doors is quite small, regarding the entirety of the building, they still contribute to overall air infiltration and the general cooling experienced on particularly windy days. Additionally, those in a poorer state are near the end of their service life given the symptoms exhibited.

Cx Associates recommends replacement of all standard swing doors, frames, and weather-stripping. Fiberglass doors, although more expensive, are durable and strong and likely to last equally as long, if not longer than a steel door. They also boast better R-values. Fiberglass doors should contain a foam-insulated core. Limit glass in doors to only where it is necessary. New doorframes should be foam-sealed into the rough opening, or per manufacturer?s recommendations, to make an airtight connection to the wall. Note that we do not recommend replacement of the aluminum entrance doors to the bakery or to the main offices, from an energy standpoint. Although the broken light at the main storefront entrance should probably be replaced as part of general upkeep. It is highly recommended that the aqua-culture door replacement be entirely a thermally broken, aluminum frame system or entirely fiberglass door and cellular PVC frame, given the high humidity environments. Wood or steel materials should not be used.

The installed Pella window units likely are not installed in a manner that would satisfy manufacturer warranty requirements, and so Cx Associates assumes any warranties available are likely voided. However, they also do not appear to show any significant signs of water infiltration at the rough openings or air leakage through the units themselves. Although it is only worth relatively minimal amounts of air leakage, it is relatively straightforward to install can-grade, single-component spray foam around all accessible rough openings where they are currently unsealed. Backer rod and caulk is a more robust air seal, but the added expense doesn?t justify the gain. That said, if can-foam is used, it is advisable to ensure that the foam is not exposed to UV, which may require trim or other means of coverage at certain locations.

column2: UVM Hills Agricultural Science Building Cx
Benefit Narrative: Multiple AHUs were specified to operate at constant 55F DAT. Supply air temperature reset was recommended and implemented.

column2: Helen Porter Healthcare and Rehabilitation Center BECx
Benefit Narrative: Functional performance testing of the window units for water penetration identified a significant manufacturer defect across all window units. In-place repairs were made under warranty during system installation. Had this issue been identified post-construction. Occupancy of rooms and overall operations would have been far more significantly affected, likely adding appreciable labor burden and operations costs to the staff and building. (INTERNAL): Cost estimate is a bit of a wild guess.

We'd commissioned the Lockwood Hotel when it was built, but during design they value-engineered out a ton of BAS integration. Sometime after our post-occ visit,  one of the DOAS unit's cooling failed and the facilities staff had no idea for a long time (they don't even know how long!). We are now working with them to provide integration recommendations for both DOAS units so that they will have more info at their fingertips both from an alarming standpoint,  but also to generally be able to monitor the units' operation.

This was a renovation project re-using some of the existing RTUs. The design included ventilation provided by the RTUs, but one of the existing units did not have OA or EA intakes. We brought this up to the owner and construction team so that the correct accessories could be installed to allow for ventilation of the served spaces.

The cold water piping entering the water heater was found to not have a strainer or check valve. These piping specialties protect the water heater against any debris in entering water that could result detrimental damage to the water heater. This could lead to costly repairs and/or the need to replace the water heater much sooner than expected. The mechanical contractor installed a combination strainer and check valve, which will protect the water heater going forward.

column2: First Church of Christ, Scientist Envelope Assessment
Benefit Narrative: Insulate currently uninsulated exterior walls to dramatically improve occupant comfort and reduce thermal heat loss and air leakage.

column2: GSA Federal Building St. Albans AHU Replacement Cx
Benefit Narrative: The coil pull space and some filter access for AHUs on this project were not adequate, and unfortunately this is how this was designed  (we didn't do DR!). We asked the team to work with the manufacturer to determine how access could be handled with the space constraints.

column2: GSA Federal Building St. Albans AHU Replacement Cx
Benefit Narrative: We have training verification in our scope, so requested training plans from contractors. The mechanical contractor did not think they owed any training on newly installed equipment. Through close review of the specs, we found where training requirements were called out for specific types of mechanical equipment. If we hadn't brought this up, the owner never would have received general training on their new air handlers, ervs, FCUs, and VAVs.

Identified an approach to apartment unit compartmentalization that ultimately provided better air tightness results than CM's prior approach and resulted in over a 50% cost savings, according to a verbal conversation with the CM. About $50,000 or a little less versus approx. $100,000

column2: Temple Sinai Vermont Ice Dam and Envelope Consulting
Benefit Narrative: Redefining the envelope of a poorly air-sealed and moderately insulated attic floor by proposing it move to the plane of the roof slope, establishing an effective air and thermal barrier while also bringing all HVAC ductwork within the conditioned envelope as well as significantly reducing impacts on seasonal ice dams.

column2: 425 Mountainside Ln. Residence Building Envelope & Design Review
Benefit Narrative: Multiple sources of air leakage were identified as a result of a qualitative infrared scan performed under negative building pressure. These leaks equate to operational energy costs if left unaddressed.

column2: 600 13th Street, NW RCx, ECM Implementation, CRREM Pathways
Benefit: Benefit Name: Decarbonization study to meet Carbon Risk Real Estate Monitor targets
Benefit Narrative: Cx Associates was commissioned by Union Investment to conduct a decarbonization study for 600 13th Street in Washington, D.C. in order to move towards a climate neutral building

column2: MMC Waldoboro Medical Office Building Cx
Benefit: Benefit Name: Estimated Energy Savings Based On Reduced Building Air Leakage Rate
Benefit Narrative: The goal for the Maine Medical Center Waldoboro Medical Office Building was to achieve the 2021 IECC air leakage rate target of 0.40 CFM/SF at 75 Pascals of pressure. Final blower door results show that the building was able to significantly exceed this target, as an air leakage rate of 0.06 CFM/SF at 75 Pascals of pressure measured. This even surpasses US 2021 Passive House Standards. This extremely low air leakage rate was able to be achieved in part thanks to a collaborate design review process  between Cx Associates and the design team, enclosure commissioning by Cx Associates which included installation verification inspections and functional performance testing, and quality installation/execution from the construction team. Based on the building's heating system, we estimate the fuel cost savings with this reduced air leakage rate when compared to the original target to be approximately $3,000 annually.

Design review identified several improvement opportunities for air barrier continuity which were necessary in order to support the projects ambitious air tightness goal of 0.07 CFM75/SF. Code minimum is 0.25 CFM75/SF
